PROFESSOR VIEW 01 / 30
General English — B2 Track — 80 Aulas

Mark Kazuyoshi Seki Omagari

From short answers to fluent debates and confident self-expression

B2 Upper-Intermediate São Paulo, SP — Morumbi Estudante — 13 anos 60 min — Presencial
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Mark Kazuyoshi Seki Omagari
13 anos
Estudante do ensino fundamental
São Paulo, SP — Morumbi
B2 (Upper-Intermediate)
General English — fluency, debates, self-expression
80 aulas de 60 min
Presencial — 2x/semana

Jornada de Transformação

De: Respostas monosilábicas e participação passiva nas aulas — sabe inglês mas não se expressa

Para: Debates e autoexpressão confiante — capaz de argumentar, defender posições e se comunicar com naturalidade em qualquer contexto

Promessa do Programa

Em 80 aulas, Mark vai transformar seu inglês passivo em uma ferramenta ativa de expressão. Vai debater, argumentar e se apresentar com confiança, conectando seus interesses (futebol, games, tecnologia) com o mundo real em inglês.

Forças

  • Vocabulário passivo amplo — entende muito mais do que fala
  • Exposição constante via games (Minecraft, FIFA) e YouTube em inglês
  • Boa compreensão auditiva de conteúdo nativo
  • Curiosidade natural e vontade de aprender
  • Faixa etária ideal para aquisição de fluência

Pontos de Melhoria

  • Produção oral limitada — respostas curtas, evita frases longas
  • Insegurança para se expressar espontaneamente
  • Confusão entre tempos verbais complexos (present perfect vs past simple)
  • Vocabulário acadêmico e de opinião ainda fraco
  • Pouca prática com discurso estruturado e argumentação

Currículo — 80 Aulas em 8 Fases

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
Phase 1: Identity & Self-Expression (Aulas 1-10)
1Beyond the Screen — Who I Really AmPresent Perfect Simple vs ContinuousIdentity debate + self-introductionRecord 2-min self-portrait
2What Makes Me DifferentMixed tenses reviewPersonality quiz discussionWrite "About Me" paragraph
3My World in WordsRelative clauses (who/which/that)Describing people and thingsDescribe 3 important people
4Things That Changed MePassive voice (present/past)Storytelling about experiencesWrite about a life-changing moment
5What Would I Do?Second conditionalHypothetical scenarios debate5 "What if" responses
6He Said, She SaidReported speechNews reporting role-playReport a conversation
7Connecting IdeasDiscourse markers (however, moreover, in addition)Structured opinion essay oralRecord opinion with connectors
8Getting Things DoneCausative have/getProblem-solving scenariosDescribe services you use
9Review & Integrate 1All Phase 1 structuresMini-presentationPrepare presentation outline
10Phase 1 ShowcaseIntegration assessmentFull presentation + Q&ASelf-reflection audio
Phase 2: Relationships & Social Dynamics (Aulas 11-20)
11Back in the DayUsed to / wouldChildhood memories discussionWrite about past habits
12I Wish Things Were DifferentWish / if onlyRegret and desire scenarios5 wish sentences
13What Could Have HappenedModal perfects (should have, could have)Analyzing past decisionsRewrite decisions
14If It Had Been DifferentThird conditionalAlternative history debateWrite 3 third conditionals
15It Was Mark Who ScoredCleft sentencesEmphasis in storytellingRewrite sentences with cleft
16It Is Said That...Passive reporting structuresNews and rumors activityReport 3 news stories
17To Play or Playing?Gerund vs infinitive (advanced)Preference debatesComplete gerund/infinitive exercises
18Could You Tell Me...?Indirect questionsPolite inquiry role-playTransform 5 direct questions
19Seeing the Game, He SmiledParticiple clausesComplex sentence buildingWrite 5 participle sentences
20Phase 2 ShowcaseIntegration assessmentSocial scenario simulationSelf-reflection audio
Phase 3: Pop Culture & Media Literacy (Aulas 21-30)
21Once Upon a Time OnlineNarrative tensesStory retelling from mediaRetell a movie plot
22If I Had Known + Now I KnowMixed conditionalsAnalyzing character decisionsMixed conditional analysis
23What Will Happen Next?Future forms contrast (will/going to/present continuous)Predictions and plans debateWrite predictions for a series
24Not Only... But AlsoInversion for emphasisFormal review writingWrite a formal review
25The Game, a Game, GamesArticles (advanced usage)Article analysis in textsFill-in articles exercise
26The Director Insisted That...Reporting verbs (suggest, insist, deny)Entertainment news reportingReport celebrity interviews
27What Matters Is...Noun clausesOpinion structuringWrite complex opinions
28Some People Think So, Others Do NotEllipsis and substitutionDebate practiceRecord a debate argument
29Although It Seems Easy...Concessive clausesBalanced argument writingWrite a balanced review
30Phase 3 ShowcaseIntegration assessmentMedia review presentationSelf-reflection audio
Phase 4: Travel & The World (Aulas 31-40)
31By Then I Will Have...Future perfectTravel planning discussionPlan a trip itinerary
32Make Sure You...Complex imperatives and instructionsTravel advice role-playWrite travel tips
33Because of the Rain...Cause and result clausesTravel problem-solvingDescribe cause-effect situations
34Hardly Any, a Great DealAdvanced quantifiersComparing travel experiencesCompare two destinations
35The Trip Was Organized By...Advanced passive constructionsTravel narrativeWrite a passive travel report
36You Have Been There, Have You Not?Question tagsConversational practiceAdd question tags to sentences
37I Should Have Packed...Past modals reviewTravel regrets discussionWrite about travel mistakes
38I Suggest He Go...SubjunctiveFormal recommendationsWrite formal travel advice
39At This Time Tomorrow...Future continuous and future perfectFuture plans discussionDescribe future scenarios
40Phase 4 ShowcaseIntegration assessmentTravel presentationSelf-reflection audio
Phase 5: Technology & Innovation (Aulas 41-50)
41The AI That Changed EverythingComplex noun phrasesTech article discussionSummarize a tech article
42This Leads To...Cause and effect structuresTech impact analysisWrite cause-effect chains
43You Must, You Should, You CouldModals of obligation and recommendationDigital ethics debateWrite recommendations
44Similarly, In Contrast...Comparing and contrasting structuresProduct comparisonCompare two tech products
45The Game Which I Play vs The Game I PlayDefining vs non-defining relative clausesDescription precision practiceWrite both clause types
46Conditionals MasterclassAll conditionals reviewConditional chain gameWrite conditional chains
47Despite That, NeverthelessConcession and contrastDebate with counter-argumentsStructure a debate argument
48It Is Precisely This That...Emphatic structuresPersuasive speech practiceWrite a persuasive paragraph
49Convince MePersuasive languagePitch presentationPrepare a product pitch
50Phase 5 ShowcaseIntegration assessmentTech pitch presentationSelf-reflection audio
Phase 6: Global Issues & Opinions (Aulas 51-60)
51If It Were Not For...Complex conditionalsGlobal issues discussionWrite complex conditionals
52It Is Believed That...Passive reporting (advanced)News analysisReport global news
53It Could Be Argued That...Hedging languageAcademic discussion practiceHedge 5 strong opinions
54Far More Important Than...Advanced comparison structuresPriority ranking debateWrite comparisons
55Having Considered the Evidence...Participle clauses (advanced)Structured argumentationWrite complex sentences
56On the Other Hand...Argumentation structuresFormal debatePrepare debate notes
57While I Understand Your Point...Counter-argument languageResponding to oppositionWrite counter-arguments
58The Implementation of...NominalisationFormal writing practiceNominalise 10 sentences
59Formal vs InformalFormal registerRegister switching exerciseRewrite informal to formal
60Phase 6 ShowcaseIntegration assessmentFormal debate showcaseSelf-reflection audio
Phase 7: Academic & Professional Skills (Aulas 61-70)
61Building a Strong ParagraphTopic sentences and cohesionParagraph writing workshopWrite 3 structured paragraphs
62First, Next, FinallySignposting languagePresentation structure practiceOutline a presentation
63Is This a Good Argument?Evaluating argumentsCritical thinking exerciseEvaluate 3 arguments
64Let Me Tell You About MyselfSelf-presentation languageInterview simulationRecord self-presentation
65Casual to Formal in 3 StepsRegister switchingMulti-register scenariosWrite same message 3 ways
66If We Could Agree On...Negotiation conditionalsNegotiation role-playWrite negotiation dialogue
67In Other Words...Paraphrasing skillsParaphrasing challengeParaphrase 5 texts
68Across CulturesIntercultural communicationCross-cultural scenariosResearch cultural differences
69Brand YouSelf-marketing languagePersonal brand presentationCreate personal brand statement
70Phase 7 ShowcaseIntegration assessmentProfessional showcaseSelf-reflection audio

71Tense MasterclassAll tenses reviewTense accuracy challengeMixed tense exercises

77Common B2 ErrorsError correction focusError hunt and correctionCorrect error-filled text

Welcome, Mark!

Onboarding

Welcome to your program! Before each lesson, complete the Pre-class activities. Here are 5 useful phrases you can use anytime:

1 What I mean is...
2 I see your point, but I would argue that...
3 That is a fair point.
4 Could you explain what you mean by that?
5 Let me put it another way.
Aula 01 — Pre-class

Beyond the Screen — Who I Really Am

Identity, self-expression, and the Present Perfect Simple vs Continuous
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary

Learn these 10 key words and expressions. Click Listen to hear each one.

stand out • to be easily noticed because of being different or better
"He always stands out because of his unique style."
figure out • to understand or solve something after thinking carefully
"I need to figure out how to beat this level."
keep up with • to move or progress at the same speed as someone or something
"It is hard to keep up with all the new updates."
come across as • to give others a particular impression of yourself
"She comes across as very confident in interviews."
open-minded • willing to consider new and different ideas
"Being open-minded helps you learn from different cultures."
competitive • having a strong desire to win or be better than others
"He is very competitive when it comes to FIFA matches."
perspective • a particular way of thinking about something
"Traveling gives you a new perspective on life."
underestimate • to think someone or something is less capable than they really are
"Never underestimate a quiet person."
self-conscious • feeling worried about how others perceive you
"Many teenagers feel self-conscious about speaking in public."
break through • to overcome a barrier or challenge successfully
"She finally broke through her fear of public speaking."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word to its correct definition.

stand out
perspective
open-minded
underestimate
break through

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Read the text about Mark and answer the questions below.

Mark has lived in São Paulo his entire life. He has been playing soccer since he was five years old, and it is still his biggest passion. Recently, he has started to explore competitive gaming, and he has been spending more time on FIFA tournaments with friends online. He has always been a quiet person, but lately he has been trying to express himself more in English. He has watched hundreds of YouTube videos in English, which has helped him understand native speakers better. However, he has not had many opportunities to practice speaking, so he has been looking for ways to break through that barrier.

1. Which sentence uses the Present Perfect Continuous?
A "He has lived in São Paulo his entire life."
B "He has been playing soccer since he was five."
C "He has watched hundreds of YouTube videos."
2. Why does the text use "has started" (Simple) instead of "has been starting"?
A Because it refers to a completed action with a present result
B Because the action is still happening right now
C Because it happened at a specific time in the past
3. "He has been looking for ways to break through." This tells us that Mark...
A already found a solution
B stopped looking for solutions
C is still in the process of searching

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Present Perfect Simple vs Continuous

Present Perfect Simple — focuses on the result or completion of an action.

FormStructureExample
+Subject + have/has + past participleI have played FIFA since 2020.
-Subject + have/has + not + past participleHe has not finished the project.
?Have/Has + subject + past participleHave you seen this movie?

Present Perfect Continuous — focuses on the duration or ongoing nature of an action.

FormStructureExample
+Subject + have/has + been + verb-ingI have been playing for three hours.
-Subject + have/has + not + been + verb-ingHe has not been studying enough.
?Have/Has + subject + been + verb-ingHave you been waiting long?
Key Difference

Simple = How many? How much? (result/quantity)
"I have read five books this month." (focus on the number)

Continuous = How long? (duration/process)
"I have been reading all morning." (focus on the ongoing activity)

Note: State verbs (know, believe, want, like) do NOT use the continuous form. "I have known him for years." (NOT "I have been knowing")

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form of the verb.

"I soccer since I was five years old." (play)
"She hundreds of videos in English." (watch)
"We for thirty minutes." (wait)
"He sushi before." (never / try)
"They for the tournament all week." (practice)

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Arrange these sentences to create a coherent self-introduction.

? "I have been playing soccer since I was five, and I have also been getting into competitive FIFA."
? "Hi, my name is Mark. I am thirteen years old and I live in Morumbi, São Paulo."
? "I would say I am open-minded and competitive, and I want to break through and express myself better."
? "I think what makes me stand out is that I am a mix of Japanese and Brazilian culture."
? "Some people underestimate me because I am quiet, but I have been working on expressing my perspective more."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I have been playing soccer since I was five years old, and it is still my biggest passion.
Some people underestimate me because I come across as quiet, but I have a lot to say.
I have been trying to break through my comfort zone and share my perspective more openly.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. A new student joins your class and asks you to describe yourself. What is the best response?
A "I am Mark. I like things."
B "I am Mark. I have been living in Morumbi my whole life, and I am really into soccer and gaming."
C "I am a student. I go to school."
2. Your friend says "You are always so quiet in class." How do you respond using vocabulary from this lesson?
A "Yes, I know. Sorry."
B "I do not care what you think."
C "I used to be self-conscious about it, but I have been trying to share my perspective more."
3. Someone says "Gaming is a waste of time." How do you disagree respectfully?
A "I see your point, but I would argue that gaming helps you figure out strategies and keep up with technology."
B "No, you are wrong."
C "Maybe."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are introducing yourself to an international exchange student who has never met you. In 60 seconds, tell them: Who are you beyond what people see? What have you been doing lately? What makes you stand out? Use at least 3 vocabulary words from this lesson.

Survival Card — Lesson 1

1 What I mean is...
2 I see your point, but I would argue that...
3 That is a fair point.
4 Could you explain what you mean by that?
5 Let me put it another way.
Aula 02 — Pre-class

What Makes Me Tick

Personality collocations and mixed tenses review
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary

Learn these 10 key words and expressions. Click Listen to hear each one.

driven•very determined and motivated to succeed
"She is a very driven person who always achieves her goals."
laid-back•relaxed and not easily worried about things
"My dad is really laid-back about most things."
overthink•to think about something too much or for too long
"I tend to overthink simple decisions."
get along with•to have a friendly relationship with someone
"I get along with most people at school."
look up to•to admire and respect someone
"I really look up to my older brother."
personality•the set of qualities that make a person different from others
"He has a great personality and everyone likes him."
trait•a particular quality in someone's character
"Honesty is one of his best traits."
ambitious•having a strong desire to achieve something important
"She is ambitious and wants to study abroad."
empathetic•able to understand and share the feelings of others
"Empathetic people make great friends."
shape (verb)•to influence the way something develops
"Your experiences shape who you become."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
laid-back
driven
look up to
overthink
trait

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Mark has always been a quiet person, but lately he has been trying to open up more. Last year, he joined a debate club at school, and since then, he has participated in three competitions. He was nervous at first, but his teacher told him that practice shapes confidence. Mark is driven and ambitious, even though he sometimes overthinks his responses. He gets along with his classmates and looks up to his debate coach, who he considers a great role model.

1. Which tense is used to describe Mark's personality traits that are still true?
A Past Simple
B Present Simple
C Present Perfect Continuous
2. Why does the text use "has been trying" instead of "tried"?
A Because it happened once in the past
B Because the action started in the past and continues now
C Because the action is finished
3. Which sentence uses Past Simple correctly?
A He has joined a debate club last year.
B He joined a debate club last year.
C He was joining a debate club last year.

Grammar Tip: Mixed Tenses Review

Grammar

When talking about personality and experiences, we often mix tenses:

TenseUseExample
Present SimplePermanent traitsHe is driven.
Past SimpleCompleted past actionsHe joined the club last year.
Present PerfectPast actions with present relevanceHe has participated in three debates.
Present Perfect ContinuousActions that started in the past and continueHe has been trying to be more confident.

Key rule: Use Past Simple with specific time markers (yesterday, last year). Use Present Perfect when the time is not important or the action connects to now.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice
She is very and never gives up.
I all my teammates.
He tends to every decision.
Patience is an important personality .
Your experiences who you become.

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put these sentences in the correct order to form a logical paragraph about personality.

? However, he has been working on being more outgoing.
? Mark has always been a laid-back person.
? Now he gets along with everyone in his class.
? He used to overthink everything before speaking.
? His ambition and empathy have shaped him into a better communicator.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
I have always been a driven person, and that trait has shaped who I am today.
I get along with most people because I am empathetic and laid-back.
I look up to people who are ambitious but also kind and supportive.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
A friend asks you to describe your best quality. What do you say?
A "I am being empathetic since I was born."
B "I think my best trait is that I am empathetic. I have always tried to understand other people."
C "I have empathetic since forever."
Your teacher asks: "How have you changed this year?" What is the best response?
A "I have been trying to be less laid-back and more driven in my studies."
B "I tried to be less laid-back and more driven yesterday."
C "I am trying since January."
Someone asks who you look up to. How do you respond?
A "I am looking up to my coach since I was ten."
B "I look up my coach."
C "I look up to my coach. He has always been ambitious and supportive."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Think about someone you look up to. What personality traits do they have? How have they shaped who you are? Record your answer using at least 4 vocabulary words from this lesson.

Survival Card — Lesson 2

1I would describe myself as...
2One of my strongest traits is...
3I have always been the kind of person who...
4I think my personality has been shaped by...
5People who know me well would say I am...
Aula 03 — Pre-class

The People Around Me

Relative clauses (defining and non-defining) and describing people
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary

Learn these 10 key words and expressions. Click Listen to hear each one.

bond•a strong connection or relationship between people
"The bond between the two friends is unbreakable."
sibling•a brother or sister
"Do you have any siblings?"
get on someone's nerves•to annoy or irritate someone
"My little brother really gets on my nerves sometimes."
take after•to look or behave like an older relative
"She takes after her mother in many ways."
count on•to depend on someone, to trust them to help
"You can always count on me."
acquaintance•someone you know but who is not a close friend
"He is just an acquaintance from school, not a close friend."
close-knit•having strong, close relationships within a group
"We are a very close-knit family."
fall out with•to have an argument and stop being friendly
"I fell out with my best friend over something silly."
supportive•giving help, encouragement, or emotional support
"My parents are very supportive of my goals."
role model•a person whose behavior is imitated by others
"Athletes can be great role models for young people."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
sibling
get on someone's nerves
count on
fall out with
acquaintance

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Mark, who is 13 years old, has a close-knit family. His older brother, who he takes after, is his biggest role model. The friends that he counts on the most are the ones from his soccer team. He also has school acquaintances who he gets along with, but the bond that he shares with his teammates is much deeper. Last month, Mark fell out with a classmate who had been getting on his nerves for weeks. His mother, who is always supportive, helped him work through the situation.

1. In the text, "who is 13 years old" is an example of:
A A defining relative clause (essential information)
B A non-defining relative clause (extra information)
C A conditional clause
2. "The friends that he counts on the most" uses a defining clause because:
A It specifies which friends we are talking about
B It adds extra information about all friends
C It describes Mark's personality
3. How can you tell the difference between defining and non-defining relative clauses in writing?
A Defining clauses always use "who"
B Non-defining clauses are separated by commas
C There is no difference in writing

Grammar Tip: Relative Clauses

Grammar

We use relative clauses to give more information about a noun:

TypePurposeExample
DefiningEssential info (no commas)The friend who I count on is Leo.
Non-definingExtra info (with commas)Leo, who is my best friend, plays soccer.
who / thatFor peopleThe person who/that I admire...
which / thatFor thingsThe game which/that I play...
whosePossessionThe friend whose brother plays...

Important: You cannot use "that" in non-defining relative clauses. Only "who," "which," or "whose."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice
My sister, lives in Tokyo, is very supportive.
The people I count on are my family.
He has a friend father is a famous soccer player.
She is the kind of person never falls out with anyone.
Soccer, is his favorite sport, has given him many close friends.

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
?However, we sometimes get on each other's nerves over silly things.
?He is someone who I can always count on.
?My brother, who is two years older than me, is my closest sibling.
?Despite that, the bond that we share is stronger than any argument.
?We have a close-knit relationship and share many interests.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
My cousin Ana, who lives nearby, is someone I can always count on.
The friends that I have the strongest bond with are the ones from my soccer team.
My mother, who is my biggest role model, is incredibly supportive.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
You want to describe your best friend without giving extra details. Which is correct?
A "The person who I trust the most is my best friend Leo."
B "The person, who I trust the most, is my best friend Leo."
C "The person which I trust the most is my best friend Leo."
You want to add extra information about your sibling. Which is correct?
A "My brother that is 15 is a great soccer player."
B "My brother, who is 15, is a great soccer player."
C "My brother, that is 15, is a great soccer player."
How would you describe a family using "close-knit" in a relative clause?
A "My family, which is close-knit, we always have dinner together."
B "My family, which is very close-knit, always has dinner together."
C "My family that close-knit always has dinner together."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Describe the people around you. Talk about your family, friends, and acquaintances. Use at least 3 relative clauses (defining and non-defining) and vocabulary from this lesson.

Survival Card — Lesson 3

1The person who I trust the most is...
2We have a really close-knit relationship.
3I take after my father in many ways.
4She is someone who I can always count on.
5We fell out over something silly, but we made up.
Aula 04 — Pre-class

Online vs Offline

Passive voice (present and past) and digital life vocabulary
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary

Learn these 10 key words and expressions. Click Listen to hear each one.

curate•to select and organize content carefully
"Social media feeds are curated by algorithms."
algorithm•a set of rules that a computer follows to solve a problem
"The algorithm decides what videos you see first."
filter•to remove unwanted things, or a tool that changes appearance
"Many people use filters to change how they look online."
scroll through•to move through content on a screen by swiping or sliding
"I spend too much time scrolling through my phone."
log off•to stop using a computer system or social media
"Sometimes you just need to log off and go outside."
digital footprint•the trail of data you leave behind when using the internet
"Your digital footprint can affect your future opportunities."
authentic•genuine and real, not fake or copied
"It is important to be authentic on social media."
disconnect•to stop being connected, especially to the internet
"I like to disconnect from technology on weekends."
screen time•the amount of time spent looking at a screen
"My screen time last week was over six hours a day."
perception•the way something is understood or interpreted by others
"Social media can change people's perception of reality."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
algorithm
digital footprint
authentic
screen time
disconnect

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Every day, billions of posts are shared on social media. Content is curated by algorithms that decide what you see. Your feed is filtered to show you things you might like. Last year, a study was published showing that teenagers spend an average of seven hours a day on screens. The study was conducted by researchers at a university in California. It was found that too much screen time can change your perception of reality. Many experts say that digital footprints should be managed carefully because they are seen by future employers.

1. "Billions of posts are shared" is an example of:
A Present passive
B Past passive
C Active voice
2. Why is the passive voice used in "A study was published"?
A Because we want to emphasize who published it
B Because the action (the study) is more important than who did it
C Because the sentence is about the past
3. Which sentence correctly uses present passive?
A "Algorithms are curate content."
B "Content is curated by algorithms."
C "Content was curated by algorithms." (when talking about a general fact)

Grammar Tip: Passive Voice

Grammar

We use the passive when the action is more important than who does it:

FormStructureExample
Present Passive (+)am/is/are + past participleContent is shared every day.
Present Passive (-)am/is/are + not + past participleFake news is not tolerated.
Present Passive (?)Am/Is/Are + subject + past participle?Is this content filtered?
Past Passive (+)was/were + past participleThe study was published last year.
Past Passive (-)was/were + not + past participleThe data was not deleted.
Past Passive (?)Was/Were + subject + past participle?Was the article written by AI?

Key: Active = who does it matters. Passive = what happens matters. Use "by" to mention the agent when needed.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice
Social media feeds by algorithms.
The video by millions of people.
Your digital footprint by anyone who searches your name.
The app to keep people scrolling.
Authentic content more than filtered content.

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order
?The content that is shown to you is filtered by an algorithm.
?Every day, millions of photos are shared on social media.
?Experts say we should all learn to disconnect and manage our screen time.
?Many of these photos are edited with filters before being posted.
?As a result, our perception of reality is being shaped by technology.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
Social media content is curated by algorithms that track your behavior.
I think it is important to be authentic online and manage your digital footprint.
The perception of reality is shaped by what is shown on our screens.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
A friend says "I spend too much time on my phone." How do you respond using passive voice?
A "That is normal. Most apps are designed to keep you scrolling."
B "That is normal. Most apps designed to keep you scrolling."
C "That is normal. Most apps are design to keep you scrolling."
How would you explain what happened to a viral video using past passive?
A "The video seen by millions of people."
B "The video was seen by millions of people around the world."
C "The video is seen by millions of people last week."
You want to say that people should be more real online. Which uses passive correctly?
A "Authentic content should valued more."
B "Authentic content should been valued more."
C "Authentic content should be valued more than filtered content."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Think about your online life vs your offline life. How much screen time do you have? Is the content you see curated for you? Do you think social media changes people's perception of reality? Use at least 3 passive voice sentences and vocabulary from this lesson.

Survival Card — Lesson 4

1Most content on social media is curated by algorithms.
2I think it is important to disconnect sometimes.
3Your digital footprint is seen by everyone.
4This photo was edited with a filter before it was posted.
5Being authentic online is more important than getting likes.
Aula 05 -- Pre-class

What Would I Do?

Second conditional for hypothetical scenarios and debates: dilemma, consequence, outcome, scenario, hesitate, tempting, principle, regret. Structures: "If I found..., I would...", "What would you do if...?", "If I were you...". Mission: defend your choices in the Dilemma Game.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example.

Dilemma -- a choice where every option has a problem
"Telling the truth or protecting a friend — a real dilemma."
Consequence -- the result that follows your action
"Every choice has consequences." (Consequência)
Outcome -- how a situation ends
"Nobody could predict the outcome."
Scenario -- an imagined situation, used to think or test
"Picture this scenario: you find a lost phone."
Hesitate -- to pause because you are not sure
"I would not hesitate for a second." (Hesitar)
Tempting -- attractive, even when you know it is wrong
"Keeping the money would be tempting."
Principle -- a rule you follow because you believe in it
"It is against my principles to lie." (Princípio)
Regret -- the sad feeling about something you did, or did not do
"I would regret it forever." (Arrepender-se)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its meaning.

Dilemma
Consequence
Outcome
Hesitate
Tempting
Principle

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At debate club, Sofia presents a scenario: "If you found a wallet with five hundred dollars, what would you do?" Mark does not hesitate: "Keeping it would be tempting, but I would take it to the office. If I kept it, I would regret it — that is the real consequence. And if I were rich one day, I would want people to return MY wallet. It is a question of principle."

1. Which form follows "if" in the second conditional?
A "If you would find..."
B "If you found..." (past simple)
C "If you will find..."
2. What would Mark do with the wallet?
A He would take it to the office.
B He would keep it.
C He would hide it.
3. Which form of "be" does Mark use after "if I"?
A was
B were ("If I were rich")
C am

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Second Conditional

GRAMMAR

Situações imaginárias no presente/futuro. / Imaginary situations, present or future.

PartFormExample
ConditionIf + past simpleIf I found a wallet... (Se eu encontrasse)
Resultwould + base verb...I would return it. (eu devolveria)
QuestionWhat would you do if...?What would you do if you won?
to bewere (all persons)If I were you... (Se eu fosse você)

NEVER "if + would" / "if + will". The past form creates distance from reality — it is NOT about the past.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If I a wallet, I would return it."
"What you do if you saw a friend cheating?"
"If I you, I would tell the teacher."
"Keeping the money would be ."
"Every choice has ."
"I would not for a second."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the debate in the correct order.

?Mark answers: he would return it, despite the temptation.
?Sofia presents the wallet scenario.
?Mark gives his honest final answer.
?She asks: what would you do?
?Sofia attacks with a harder scenario: the best friend.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Practice the rhythm of hypotheticals.

If I found a wallet, I would return it.
What would you do if you won a million dollars?
If I were you, I would not hesitate.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation.

A friend asks for your hypothetical opinion about quitting the team.
A "If I were you, I would talk to the coach first."
B "If I was you, I will talk to the coach."
C "If I would be you, I would talk."
The debate question is about winning the lottery.
A "If I will win, I would donate half."
B "If I won, I would donate half."
C "If I would win, I donate half."
You want to challenge your opponent with a hypothetical.
A "What you would do if you saw it?"
B "What would you do if you saw it?"
C "What would you did if you see it?"
You admit the temptation but defend your principle.
A "It would be tempting, but it is against my principles."
B "It is tempting so I would do it."
C "Temptation is my principle."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection

No right or wrong here. Think in English. Tap the mic and speak freely.

Record your answers to five "What if" questions: What would you do if you won a million dollars? If you could have any superpower? If you found a wallet full of cash? If you could change one rule at school? If you were the teacher for one day? Defend each answer with one consequence.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1What would you do if...?O que você faria se...?
2If I found it, I would return it.Se eu encontrasse, eu devolveria.
3If I were you, I would not hesitate.Se eu fosse você, eu não hesitaria.
4It would be tempting, but it is against my principles.Seria tentador, mas é contra meus princípios.
5Every choice has consequences.Toda escolha tem consequências.
Aula 06 — Pre-class

He Said, She Said — Reported Speech

Reported speech: say vs tell and tense backshift (is→was, will→would, can→could). New vocabulary: claim, deny, headline, rumour, witness, announce, reveal, broadcast, spokesperson, according to, gossip, exaggerate.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Claim — to say that something is true, even without proof
"He claims that he scored the goal." (afirmar)
Deny — to say that something is not true or did not happen
"The club denied the story." (negar)
Headline — the title of a news story, written in big letters
"The headline said the team had won." (manchete)
Rumour — a story people repeat that may not be true
"There is a rumour that the coach will leave." (boato)
Witness — a person who saw an event and can describe it
"A witness said the stadium was full." (testemunha)
Announce — to tell people something officially and publicly
"The city announced a new centre." (anunciar)
Reveal — to make known something that was secret before
"The captain revealed his plan." (revelar)
Broadcast — to send out a programme on TV or radio
"They broadcast the final on TV." (transmitir)
Spokesperson — a person who speaks officially for a group
"A spokesperson announced the news." (porta-voz)
According to — as said or reported by a person or a source
"According to the report, the team won." (de acordo com)
Gossip — talk about other people's private lives, often unkind
"Please do not gossip about the players." (fofoca)
Exaggerate — to make something sound bigger than it really is
"Do not exaggerate the crowd size." (exagerar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Claim
Deny
Headline
Rumour
Witness
Announce
Reveal
Broadcast
Spokesperson
According to
Gossip
Exaggerate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

After the final, a reporter spoke to the coach. The coach said that the team had played well, and she told the players that she was proud of them. The captain revealed that he would stay at the club. There was a rumour that two players would leave, but a spokesperson denied it and announced that nobody was leaving. According to the report, it was the biggest crowd of the season.

1. Why do we write "he was proud" and not "he is proud"?
A Because in reported speech the tense moves one step back.
B Because "is" is always wrong.
C Because the coach is a woman.
2. Which verb is used with a person (the players)?
A said
B told
C announced
3. What did the spokesperson do with the rumour?
A Denied it and announced nobody was leaving.
B Confirmed that two players would leave.
C Said nothing about it.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Reported Speech (say, tell, backshift)

GRAMMAR

Usado para relatar o que alguém disse. O tempo verbal volta um passo. / Used to report what someone said; the tense moves one step back.

Direct / DiretoReported / RelatadoChange / Mudança
"I am tired"He said he was tiredam/is → was
"We will win"She said they would winwill → would
"I can play"He said he could playcan → could
"He told me..."tell + person; say + no persontold me / said that

Key point / Ponto-chave: use tell com uma pessoa (tell me, tell us) e say sem pessoa (say that...). Nunca diga "say me". Pronomes mudam: I → he, we → they.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I am busy" — He said he busy.
"We will win" — They said they win.
"I can play" — She said she play.
She me that the news was true.
The club the rumour.
to the report, the team won.

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?Mark tells the reporter that the coach announced the news.
?Something happens at school and Mark sees it.
?The reporter writes the headline and broadcasts the story.
?A reporter asks Mark what people said.
?Mark reports that the captain claimed the team would win.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

He said that he was tired.
Ele disse que estava cansado.
She told me that she would come.
Ela me disse que viria.
The club denied the rumour.
O clube negou o boato.
A spokesperson announced the news.
Um porta-voz anunciou a notícia.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation.

Your friend says "I will be there." How do you report it later?
A He said he will be there.
B He said he would be there.
C He said me he will be there.
Which sentence uses say and tell correctly?
A She told me that the game had started.
B She said me that the game had started.
C She told that the game had started.
A story may not be true and the club says it is false. What happens?
A The club announces the rumour.
B The club denies the rumour.
C The club exaggerates the rumour.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Be a reporter. Report three pieces of news about your favourite sport or game. Use "said that...", "told me that...", and change the tense back (is→was, will→would, can→could). Use at least three new words from this lesson.

Survival Card — Lesson 6

1He said that he was tired.Ele disse que estava cansado.
2She told me that she would come.Ela me disse que viria.
3According to the report, the team won.De acordo com o relato, o time venceu.
4The club denied the rumour.O clube negou o boato.
5A spokesperson announced the news.Um porta-voz anunciou a notícia.
Aula 07 — Pre-class

Connecting Ideas — Discourse Markers

Discourse markers to build a structured opinion: adding (moreover, in addition, furthermore), contrasting (however, on the other hand, nevertheless, despite, whereas), example (for instance), result (therefore, as a result) and concluding (in conclusion).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each connector and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

However — used to introduce a contrasting idea
"I love sports; however, I also enjoy reading." (no entanto)
Moreover — used to add a stronger supporting point
"The plan is cheap; moreover, it is fast." (além disso)
In addition — used to add another related point
"In addition, it helps the environment." (adicionalmente)
Furthermore — used to add a further, more formal point
"Furthermore, students would feel less tired." (ademais)
Therefore — used to show a result or a logical conclusion
"He studied hard; therefore, he passed." (portanto)
On the other hand — used to present the opposite side of an idea
"On the other hand, some parents disagree." (por outro lado)
Nevertheless — used to say something is true despite a problem
"It was hard; nevertheless, we finished." (mesmo assim)
For instance — used to introduce an example
"Many sports help, for instance, swimming." (por exemplo)
As a result — used to show the effect of something
"He trained daily; as a result, he improved." (como resultado)
In conclusion — used to start your final point or summary
"In conclusion, I believe schools should change." (em conclusão)
Despite — used before a noun to show contrast
"Despite the rain, we played outside." (apesar de)
Whereas — used to compare two different things or ideas
"I like tennis, whereas my brother likes football." (enquanto que)
First of all — used to introduce your very first point
"First of all, I want to explain my opinion." (antes de tudo)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each connector with its translation.

However
Moreover
In addition
Furthermore
Therefore
On the other hand
Nevertheless
For instance
As a result
In conclusion
Despite
Whereas

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the opinion text and answer the questions.

I believe our school should start one hour later. First of all, many teenagers wake up tired. Moreover, students who sleep more often get better grades. For instance, one school tried a later start and saw better results. However, some parents work early. On the other hand, the school could offer a quiet study room. Therefore, the benefits are stronger than the problems. In conclusion, a later start would help students focus.

1. Which connector adds a stronger second reason?
A Moreover
B However
C For instance
2. Which connector introduces the opposite side?
A Therefore
B However
C Moreover
3. Which connector starts the final summary?
A First of all
B For instance
C In conclusion

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Connectors by Function

GRAMMAR

Conectores organizam a opinião. Escolha pela função: adicionar, contrastar, exemplificar, concluir. / Connectors organize an opinion. Choose by function: add, contrast, give an example, conclude.

Function / FunçãoConnectorsExample
Add / Adicionarmoreover, in addition, furthermoreIt is cheap; moreover, it is fast.
Contrast / Contrastarhowever, on the other hand, whereasI like art; however, I dislike exams.
Example / Exemplificarfor instanceSome sports help, for instance, swimming.
Result / Resultadotherefore, as a resultHe trained daily; therefore, he won.
Conclude / Concluirin conclusionIn conclusion, I support the idea.

Key point / Ponto-chave: use despite antes de um substantivo (Despite the rain), e however no início de uma nova frase com vírgula (However, ...). Nunca escreva "despite of".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct connector. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I enjoy science; , I find history boring."
"The idea is cheap. , it is fast."
"He trained daily; , he improved."
"Reading is fun. In , it builds your vocabulary."
" the rain, we played outside."
"I like tennis, my brother likes football."

Stage 2: Put the Opinion in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a structured opinion in the correct order.

?Next, you add a second reason with moreover.
?First, you state your opinion clearly.
?Finally, you sum up your view with in conclusion.
?Then, you give your first reason.
?After that, you mention the other side with however.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

In my view, schools should start later.
Na minha opinião, as escolas deveriam começar mais tarde.
Moreover, students would feel less tired.
Além disso, os alunos se sentiriam menos cansados.
However, some parents disagree.
No entanto, alguns pais discordam.
In conclusion, the benefits are stronger.
Em conclusão, os benefícios são mais fortes.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best connector for each situation.

You have just given a reason and want to add a stronger one. Which word?
A Moreover
B However
C Despite
You want to show the opposite side of the argument. Which phrase?
A As a result
B On the other hand
C Furthermore
You want to give a clear example. Which phrase?
A For instance
B Therefore
C Nevertheless

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Give your structured opinion: "Should teenagers do team sports at school?" State your view, give two reasons with moreover and in addition, mention the other side with however or on the other hand, and finish with in conclusion. Use at least four connectors from this lesson.

Survival Card — Lesson 7

1In my view, schools should start later.Na minha opinião, as escolas deveriam começar mais tarde.
2Moreover, students would feel less tired.Além disso, os alunos se sentiriam menos cansados.
3However, some parents disagree.No entanto, alguns pais discordam.
4For instance, sports could finish after dark.Por exemplo, os esportes poderiam terminar depois do anoitecer.
5In conclusion, the benefits are stronger.Em conclusão, os benefícios são mais fortes.
Aula 08 — Pre-class

Getting Things Done — Causative have/get

The causative (have/get something done) to describe services other people do for you, plus 12 service words: repair, replace, install, deliver, service, appointment, mechanic, plumber, tailor, technician, estimate, inspect.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each service word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Repair — to fix something that is damaged or broken
"I had my bike repaired last week." (consertar)
Replace — to put a new thing in place of an old one
"I got my cracked screen replaced." (substituir)
Install — to put equipment in place and make it ready to use
"We had new software installed." (instalar)
Deliver — to bring something to a place for someone
"They delivered the package to my door." (entregar)
Service — to check and fix a machine so it keeps working well
"We have the car serviced every year." (revisar)
Appointment — a fixed time to meet someone or use a service
"I made an appointment with the dentist." (hora marcada)
Mechanic — a person who repairs cars and machines
"The mechanic fixed the engine quickly." (mecânico)
Plumber — a person who fixes water pipes and taps
"We called a plumber for the leak." (encanador)
Tailor — a person who makes and changes clothes to fit you
"The tailor shortened my trousers." (alfaiate)
Technician — a person trained to repair technical equipment
"A technician set up the new network." (técnico)
Estimate — a guess of how much a job will cost
"The garage gave me an estimate." (orçamento)
Inspect — to look at something carefully to check its condition
"They inspected the roof for damage." (inspecionar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each service word with its translation.

Repair
Replace
Install
Deliver
Service
Appointment
Mechanic
Plumber
Tailor
Technician
Estimate
Inspect

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Last weekend I got many things done before my school trip. First, I had my old passport renewed at the office. Then I got my phone repaired, because the screen was cracked, and a technician also replaced the battery. On Sunday, my family had the car serviced by a mechanic, and we are having new tyres delivered this week. We did not have time to clean everything ourselves, so we are getting the house cleaned too.

1. In "I had my passport renewed", who did the work?
A The speaker
B Another person (the office)
C No one
2. Which sentence uses the causative correctly?
A I had repaired my phone.
B I got my phone repaired.
C I repaired got my phone.
3. What order do have and get follow?
A have + past participle + object
B have + object + past participle
C have + object + base verb

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — The Causative

GRAMMAR

Usamos o causativo quando OUTRA pessoa faz o trabalho para nós. Estrutura: have/get + objeto + particicio passado. / Use the causative when another person does the job for you: have/get + object + past participle.

Form / FormaStructureExample
Havehave + object + past participleI had my hair cut.
Get (informal)get + object + past participleI got my phone fixed.
Question / PerguntaDid you have/get + object + pp?Did you have your car serviced?
Negative / Negativadid not have/get + object + ppI did not get it repaired.

Key point / Ponto-chave: o objeto vem ANTES do particicio: have + my car + serviced. "I had repaired my phone" significa que VOCÊ consertou. Para serviço de outra pessoa, use "I had my phone repaired".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct past participle. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I had my bike last week."
"I got my hair yesterday."
"We have the car every year."
"She got her cracked screen ."
"We are having the house ."
"They are having the sofa ."

Stage 2: Put the Service in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of using a repair service in the correct order.

?Next, you take the item to the technician.
?First, you call the shop and explain the problem.
?Finally, you collect it and pay for the service.
?Then, you make an appointment for the repair.
?After that, you have it fixed or replaced.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I had my phone repaired.
Eu mandei consertar meu telefone.
I got my hair cut yesterday.
Eu cortei o cabelo ontem (com um barbeiro).
We are having the house painted.
Estamos mandando pintar a casa.
Did you get your watch fixed?
Você mandou consertar seu relógio?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best causative sentence for each situation.

A barber cut your hair. How do you say it?
A I had my hair cut.
B I had cut my hair.
C I cut my hair done.
A technician will repair your laptop. Which is correct?
A I will repair my laptop.
B I will have my laptop repaired.
C I will have repaired my laptop.
Choose the more informal version of "I had my car serviced."
A I got my car serviced.
B I serviced my car.
C I had serviced my car.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe three things you or your family had done last month. Use have or get + object + past participle (for example: "I had my phone repaired"). Use at least three service words from this lesson, such as mechanic, technician or appointment.

Survival Card — Lesson 8

1I had my phone repaired.Eu mandei consertar meu telefone.
2I got my hair cut yesterday.Eu cortei o cabelo ontem (com um barbeiro).
3I need to have my car serviced.Preciso fazer a revisão do meu carro.
4We are having the sofa delivered.Estamos recebendo o sofá em casa (entrega).
5Did you get your watch fixed?Você mandou consertar seu relógio?
Aula 09 — Pre-class

Bring It All Together — Mini-presentation

Deliver a short, clear mini-presentation: signpost language (Let me start with, Moving on to, To sum up) plus 12 presentation words: audience, outline, rehearse, takeaway, visual aid, engage, confident, pace, recap, highlight, nervous, transition.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each presentation word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Audience — the group of people who listen to your talk
"The audience clapped at the end." (plateia)
Outline — a short plan that shows the main parts of your talk
"I wrote an outline before I started." (roteiro)
Rehearse — to practice your talk out loud before you give it
"I rehearsed three times at home." (ensaiar)
Takeaway — the main idea you want the audience to remember
"My takeaway is: keep practicing." (mensagem principal)
Visual aid — a picture, slide or object that supports your talk
"My visual aid was one simple slide." (recurso visual)
Engage — to hold the interest and attention of your audience
"Eye contact helps you engage people." (cativar)
Confident — sure of yourself; not afraid to speak
"She looked calm and confident on stage." (confiante)
Pace — the speed at which you speak during a talk
"Slow your pace so people follow you." (ritmo)
Recap — to say the main points again, in short, at the end
"Let me recap the three key ideas." (recapitular)
Highlight — to draw attention to an important idea
"I want to highlight one key point." (destacar)
Nervous — worried or anxious, especially before speaking
"I felt nervous, then I took a breath." (nervoso)
Transition — a smooth change from one part of a talk to the next
"A good transition links your ideas." (transição)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each presentation word with its translation.

Audience
Outline
Rehearse
Takeaway
Visual aid
Engage
Confident
Pace
Recap
Highlight
Nervous
Transition

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Next week Mark gives his first mini-presentation. He felt nervous, so he wrote a clear outline and prepared one simple visual aid. He opened with a signpost: "Let me start with a short story." He used a smooth transition to his second idea: "Moving on to the main reason." In the middle he said, "I would like to highlight one key point," and he watched his pace so the audience could follow. At the end he said, "To sum up, let me recap the three ideas," and gave one clear takeaway. He rehearsed three times, so he felt calm and confident.

1. Which phrase signals the OPENING of the talk?
A Let me start with...
B To sum up...
C Moving on to...
2. Why did Mark watch his pace?
A To finish faster
B So the audience could follow
C To skip the outline
3. What did rehearsing three times help him feel?
A More nervous
B Calm and confident
C Bored

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Signpost Language

GRAMMAR

Numa mini-apresentação, usamos frases de sinalização (signpost language) para guiar o público de uma parte para a outra. / In a mini-presentation, use signpost phrases to guide your audience from one part to the next.

Stage / EtapaSignpost phraseUse
Opening / AberturaLet me start with...introduce the first idea
Transition / TransiçãoMoving on to...move to the next idea
Emphasis / ÊnfaseI would like to highlight...stress one key point
Closing / FechamentoTo sum up...recap and give the takeaway

Key point / Ponto-chave: signpost phrases são como placas na estrada: dizem ao público onde você está e para onde vai. Comece com "Let me start with", mude de tema com "Moving on to", e termine com "To sum up" + um takeaway claro.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct presentation word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Before I started, I wrote a short ."
"I my talk three times."
"Slow your so people can follow."
"I want to one key point."
"To sum up, let me the key ideas."
"My main is to keep practicing."

Stage 2: Put the Talk in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of preparing a mini-presentation in the correct order.

?Next, you rehearse your talk out loud and check your pace.
?First, you choose your topic and write a short outline.
?Finally, you present with confidence and recap your key message.
?Then, you create one simple visual aid to support your idea.
?After that, you practice your signpost phrases and your main takeaway.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each signpost phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Let me start with a short outline.
Deixe-me começar com um breve roteiro.
Moving on to my next point.
Passando para o meu próximo ponto.
I would like to highlight one key idea.
Eu gostaria de destacar uma ideia principal.
To sum up, my takeaway is simple.
Para resumir, minha mensagem principal é simples.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best signpost phrase for each moment of your talk.

You are about to introduce your first idea. What do you say?
A Let me start with my background.
B To sum up my background.
C That is all about my background.
You want to move from idea one to idea two. What do you say?
A In conclusion, my second idea is...
B Moving on to my second idea...
C Let me start with my second idea...
You are ending your talk and want one clear message. What do you say?
A Moving on, my takeaway is...
B To sum up, my takeaway is...
C Let me start with my takeaway...

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Plan a one-minute mini-presentation about something you love. Open with "Let me start with...", use "Moving on to..." for a transition, and close with "To sum up..." and one clear takeaway. Try to use at least four words from this lesson, such as outline, audience, pace or confident.

Survival Card — Lesson 9

1Let me start with a short outline.Deixe-me começar com um breve roteiro.
2Moving on to my next point.Passando para o meu próximo ponto.
3I would like to highlight one key idea.Eu gostaria de destacar uma ideia principal.
4To sum up, my takeaway is simple.Para resumir, minha mensagem principal é simples.
5Let me recap the three key ideas.Deixe-me recapitular as três ideias principais.
Aula 10 — Pre-class

Take the Stage — Phase 1 Showcase

Handle a question-and-answer session with confidence: response phrases (That's a great question, Could you rephrase that, To answer your question, I'll come back to that later) plus 12 Q&A words: Q&A, clarify, address, follow-up, rephrase, acknowledge, elaborate, concise, feedback, questioner, handout, composure.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each Q&A word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Q&A — the part of a talk when the audience asks questions
"After the talk, we had a short Q&A." (sessão de perguntas)
Clarify — to make an idea or answer clearer
"Let me clarify what I mean." (esclarecer)
Address — to deal with or respond to a question or point
"I'll address your question now." (abordar)
Follow-up — an extra question asked to get more detail
"That's a good follow-up question." (pergunta complementar)
Rephrase — to say something again using different words
"Could you rephrase the question?" (reformular)
Acknowledge — to show you have heard and respect what someone said
"That's a great question, thank you." (reconhecer)
Elaborate — to give more detail about something you said
"Could you elaborate on that point?" (detalhar)
Concise — short and clear, with no extra words
"Keep your answer concise." (conciso)
Feedback — useful comments about how well you did something
"Thank you for your honest feedback." (retorno)
Questioner — the person in the audience who asks a question
"I thanked the questioner and answered." (quem pergunta)
Handout — a page you give the audience to read or keep
"I prepared a one-page handout." (folheto)
Composure — a calm, controlled state, even under pressure
"He kept his composure during the Q&A." (compostura)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each Q&A word with its translation.

Q&A
Clarify
Address
Follow-up
Rephrase
Acknowledge
Elaborate
Concise
Feedback
Questioner
Handout
Composure

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the end of his showcase, Mark invited the audience to ask questions. The first questioner asked about his routine. Mark wanted a second to think, so he said, "That's a great question," to acknowledge it. He did not catch the next question, so he asked the person to rephrase it. He kept each answer concise, and he agreed to elaborate only when someone asked a follow-up. One question was hard, but he kept his composure, smiled, and said he would find out. After the Q&A, his teacher gave him warm feedback.

1. What phrase does Mark use to acknowledge a question?
A That's a great question.
B To sum up.
C Moving on to.
2. What does Mark do when he does not catch a question?
A He ignores it.
B He asks the person to rephrase it.
C He ends the Q&A.
3. When does Mark elaborate?
A On every question.
B Only on a follow-up.
C Never.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Q&A Response Phrases

GRAMMAR

No momento das perguntas (Q&A), usamos frases fixas de resposta para manter o controle. / During the Q&A, use fixed response phrases to stay in control.

Function / FunçãoResponse phraseUse
Acknowledge / ReconhecerThat's a great question.welcome the question, buy a second
Check / VerificarCould you rephrase that?when you do not understand
Answer / ResponderTo answer your question, ...give your concise answer
Defer / AdiarI'll come back to that later.when you need more time

Key point / Ponto-chave: em inglês você ask a question (não "make"), e você answer a question (sem "to"). Essas frases de resposta não respondem sozinhas — elas te dão tempo e controle para manter a composure.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct Q&A word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"When I didn't understand, I asked her to the question."
"Let me what I mean."
"Keep your answer short and ."
"That's a great , thank you."
"I thanked the and answered."
"He stayed calm and kept his ."

Stage 2: Put the Q&A in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of handling a Q&A session in the correct order.

?Next, you acknowledge it and, if needed, ask the person to rephrase it.
?First, you finish your talk and invite the audience to ask questions.
?Finally, you thank the questioner and keep your composure until the very end.
?Then, you listen carefully to the whole question before you speak.
?After that, you give a concise answer and elaborate only if they want more.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each response phrase, then record yourself saying it.

That's a great question.
Essa é uma ótima pergunta.
Could you rephrase that, please?
Você poderia reformular isso, por favor?
To answer your question, I started small.
Respondendo à sua pergunta, eu comecei pequeno.
I kept my composure and stayed calm.
Eu mantive a compostura e fiquei calmo.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response phrase for each moment of the Q&A.

A question comes and you want a second to think. What do you say?
A That's a great question.
B That is all, thank you.
C Please make a question.
You did not understand the question. What do you say?
A I will answer later.
B Could you rephrase that, please?
C That's a great answer.
You want to give more detail on a point. What do you say?
A Let me elaborate on that.
B Let me sum up.
C Could you rephrase that?

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you just finished a short talk about something you love, and now the audience asks: "What was the hardest part?" Answer out loud. Acknowledge the question with "That's a great question," then give a concise answer and elaborate a little. Try to use at least three words from this lesson, such as acknowledge, concise, elaborate or composure.

Survival Card — Lesson 10

1That's a great question.Essa é uma ótima pergunta.
2Could you rephrase that, please?Você poderia reformular isso, por favor?
3To answer your question, I started small.Respondendo à sua pergunta, eu comecei pequeno.
4Let me elaborate on that point.Deixe-me detalhar esse ponto.
5I'm not sure, but I'll find out.Não tenho certeza, mas vou descobrir.
Aula 11 — Pre-class

Back in the Day — Childhood Memories

Talk about the past with two structures: used to (past habits AND states) and would (repeated past actions only) plus 12 memory words: nostalgia, childhood, memory, reminisce, recall, vivid, upbringing, neighborhood, generation, old-fashioned, cherish, throwback.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each memory word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Nostalgia — a warm, often happy feeling when you remember the past
"Old songs fill me with nostalgia." (nostalgia)
Childhood — the period of your life when you are a child
"I had a happy childhood." (infância)
Memory — something from the past that you remember
"That smell brings back a memory." (lembrança)
Reminisce — to talk or think about happy times in the past
"We reminisced about school." (relembrar)
Recall — to bring a memory back into your mind
"I can still recall that day." (recordar)
Vivid — very clear and detailed, like a real picture
"I have a vivid memory of it." (nítido)
Upbringing — the way your family raised and taught you
"I had a strict upbringing." (criação)
Neighborhood — the area near your home where you live
"It was a quiet neighborhood." (vizinhança)
Generation — all the people born around the same time
"My generation grew up outside." (geração)
Old-fashioned — from an earlier time; not modern
"Those games look old-fashioned now." (antiquado)
Cherish — to love and value something or someone deeply
"I cherish those summer days." (prezar)
Throwback — something that reminds you of an earlier time
"This song is a real throwback." (recordação)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each memory word with its translation.

Nostalgia
Childhood
Memory
Reminisce
Recall
Vivid
Upbringing
Neighborhood
Generation
Old-fashioned
Cherish
Throwback

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

When Mark hears an old song, he feels real nostalgia. He used to live in a small neighborhood near the city, and he has a vivid memory of those years. Used to describes how life was — he used to be a shy kid — because it works for both habits and states. For repeated actions, he uses would: every weekend, the family would visit his grandmother, and he would climb the old mango tree. He never says "I would be shy," though, because would cannot describe a state. Today Mark loves to reminisce, and he truly cherishes that part of his childhood.

1. Which structure works for both habits and states?
A used to
B would
C will
2. Why does Mark never say "I would be shy"?
A Because "would" is only for the future.
B Because "would" cannot describe a state.
C Because "shy" is not a real word.
3. What would the family do every weekend?
A They would move to a new city.
B They would visit his grandmother.
C They would sell the mango tree.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — used to & would

GRAMMAR

Para falar de hábitos e estados do passado que não são mais verdade, o inglês usa "used to" e "would". / To talk about past habits and states that are no longer true, English uses "used to" and "would".

StructureUse / UsoExample
used to + verbhabits AND states / hábitos E estadosI used to live there. / I used to be shy.
would + verbrepeated actions only / só ações repetidasWe would play outside every day.
didn't use to / Did you use to?negative & questions / negativa e perguntasI didn't use to like it. / Did you use to play?

Key point / Ponto-chave: "used to" serve para tudo (ações e estados); "would" serve só para ações repetidas, NUNCA para estados — por isso dizemos "I used to be shy", nunca "I would be shy". No afirmativo é sempre "used to" (com -d); na negativa e perguntas, "use to" (sem -d).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"When I was a kid, I be very shy."
"Every summer, we visit my grandparents."
"I have a very clear and memory of it."
"I grew up in a quiet ."
"I love to about old times."
"I really those memories."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of sharing a childhood memory in the correct order.

?Next, you describe a habit, like the games you would play after school.
?First, you choose one clear memory from your childhood.
?Finally, you say how that memory makes you feel today.
?Then, you set the scene and say where you used to live.
?After that, you add a vivid detail, a sound, a color, or a smell.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I used to live in a small neighborhood.
Eu morava em uma pequena vizinhança.
Every weekend, we would go fishing.
Todo fim de semana, nós íamos pescar.
I used to be really shy.
Eu era muito tímido.
I really cherish those memories.
Eu prezo muito por essas lembranças.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct way to talk about the past.

You want to say you were a shy child (a past state). Which is correct?
A I used to be shy.
B I would be shy.
C I use to be shy.
You want to describe a repeated childhood action. Which is most natural?
A We would play soccer every day.
B We would be soccer players.
C We will play soccer every day.
Which negative sentence is correct?
A I didn't used to like it.
B I didn't use to like it.
C I didn't would like it.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about your childhood and record a short story out loud. Where did you use to live, and what would you do for fun? Add one vivid detail, like a sound, a color, or a smell. Try to use at least three words from this lesson, such as childhood, vivid, neighborhood or cherish, and use both "used to" and "would".

Survival Card — Lesson 11

1I used to live in a small neighborhood.Eu morava em uma pequena vizinhança.
2Every weekend, we would visit my grandmother.Todo fim de semana, nós visitávamos minha avó.
3I used to be a really shy kid.Eu era uma criança muito tímida.
4I have a vivid memory of that place.Eu tenho uma lembrança nítida daquele lugar.
5I really cherish those memories.Eu prezo muito por essas lembranças.
Aula 12 — Pre-class

I Wish Things Were Different — Wishes & Regrets

Talk about wishes and regrets: wish + past simple (about now), wish + past perfect (regret about the past), wish + would (annoying habits) and if only, plus 12 wish words: desire, longing, crave, hindsight, daydream, hypothetical, content, discontent, otherwise, settle for, wishful, grateful.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each wish word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Desire — a strong feeling of wanting something
"She has a strong desire to travel." (desejo)
Longing — a deep, often emotional wish for something
"He felt a longing for home." (anseio)
Crave — to want something very strongly
"I crave a quiet weekend." (ansiar por)
Hindsight — understanding of an event only after it happens
"In hindsight, I should have stayed." (retrospecto)
Daydream — to imagine pleasant things instead of focusing
"He likes to daydream in class." (devanear)
Hypothetical — based on a situation that is imagined, not real
"That is just a hypothetical case." (hipotético)
Content — happy and satisfied with what you have
"I feel content with my life." (satisfeito)
Discontent — a feeling of being unhappy and wanting change
"There was deep discontent at work." (insatisfação)
Otherwise — in a different way; or, if the situation were different
"Hurry, otherwise we will be late." (caso contrário)
Settle for — to accept something that is less than you wanted
"Do not settle for less than your dream." (contentar-se com)
Wishful — hoping for something that is unlikely to happen
"That is just wishful thinking." (iludido)
Grateful — feeling thankful for something good in your life
"I am grateful for my friends." (grato)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each wish word with its translation.

Desire
Longing
Crave
Hindsight
Daydream
Hypothetical
Content
Discontent
Otherwise
Settle for
Wishful
Grateful

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Mark has a strong desire to play the guitar. When he talks about now, he says I wish I had more free time — with the past simple, because the wish is about the present. With the verb to be, he uses were: I wish I were more patient. When he thinks about the past, he uses the past perfect: in hindsight, I wish I had started earlier. To complain about a habit, he uses would: I wish my brother would turn off the lights. He tries not to settle for wishful thinking. Instead, he stays grateful and takes action.

1. Which tense follows "wish" for a wish about now?
A the past simple
B the present simple
C the future
2. How does Mark express a regret about the past?
A with "wish" plus the present simple
B with "wish" plus the past perfect
C with "wish" plus "will"
3. Which form does he use with the verb to be?
A I wish I was
B I wish I were
C I wish I would be

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — wish, would & if only

GRAMMAR

Para falar de desejos e arrependimentos, o inglês usa "wish" e "if only", e move o verbo um passo para trás. / To talk about wishes and regrets, English uses "wish" and "if only", moving the verb one step back in time.

StructureUse / UsoExample
wish + past simplewish about NOW / desejo sobre agoraI wish I had more time. / I wish I were taller.
wish + past perfectregret about the PAST / arrependimentoI wish I had studied harder.
wish + wouldannoying habit / hábito irritante de outra pessoaI wish you would stop interrupting.
if onlystronger emphasis / mais ênfaseIf only I had known!

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de "wish" o verbo recua um tempo: presente vira past simple (I wish I had), passado vira past perfect (I wish I had had). Um wish descreve o oposto da verdade. Com o verbo "to be", use were para todos: "I wish I were taller", nunca "I wish I was taller".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I wish I more free time."
"I wish I taller."
"In , I should have spoken up."
"I have a strong to learn the guitar."
"I am very for everything I have."
"I wish my brother clean his room."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of expressing a wish in the correct order.

?Next, for a regret about the past, you use wish plus the past perfect.
?First, you decide if your wish is about now or about the past.
?Finally, you explain why you feel that way, so the wish makes sense.
?Then, for the present, you use wish plus the past simple.
?After that, you can add if only for stronger emphasis.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I wish I had more free time.
Eu queria ter mais tempo livre.
I wish I were more patient.
Eu queria ser mais paciente.
I wish I had started earlier.
Eu queria ter começado mais cedo.
If only I had known sooner.
Se eu tivesse sabido antes.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct way to express each wish.

You do not have a car and want one now. Which is correct?
A I wish I had a car.
B I wish I have a car.
C I wish I will have a car.
You regret not studying last year. Which is most natural?
A I wish I studied harder.
B I wish I had studied harder.
C I wish I would study harder.
You want to say you are not tall enough, using "to be". Which is correct?
A I wish I would be taller.
B I wish I were taller.
C I wish I am taller.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about your own wishes and record a short answer out loud. Share one wish about now (I wish I had... or I wish I were...) and one regret about the past (I wish I had...). Then say what you will actually do about it. Try to use at least three words from this lesson, such as desire, hindsight, grateful or settle for.

Survival Card — Lesson 12

1I wish I had more free time.Eu queria ter mais tempo livre.
2I wish I were more patient.Eu queria ser mais paciente.
3I wish I had started earlier.Eu queria ter começado mais cedo.
4If only I had known sooner.Se eu tivesse sabido antes.
5Still, I am grateful for what I have.Ainda assim, sou grato pelo que tenho.
Aula 13 — Pre-class

What Could Have Happened — Modal Perfects

Look back at the past with modal perfects: should have (criticism), could have (a missed possibility), might/may have (a guess) and would have (an imagined result), plus 12 look-back words: aftermath, setback, overlook, foresee, reckless, cautious, backfire, bound to, spare, pull off, worthwhile, inevitable.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each look-back word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Aftermath — the situation that results from a bad event
"The aftermath of the storm was hard." (consequências)
Setback — a problem that delays or reverses progress
"The bad grade was a real setback." (revés)
Overlook — to miss or fail to notice something
"I overlooked one small detail." (deixar passar)
Foresee — to know or expect something before it happens
"No one could foresee the result." (prever)
Reckless — acting without thinking about the danger
"That was a reckless decision." (imprudente)
Cautious — careful, trying to avoid problems or danger
"She is cautious with money." (cauteloso)
Backfire — to have the opposite effect to the one you wanted
"The plan backfired badly." (sair pela culatra)
Bound to — almost certain to happen or be true
"Hard work is bound to pay off." (fadado a)
Spare — to make time or something available to give or use
