"Can you spare an hour today?" (dispor de)
Pull off — to succeed at something difficult or unexpected
"He pulled off a great result." (conseguir)
Worthwhile — worth the time, money or effort you put in
"It was a worthwhile lesson." (que vale a pena)
Inevitable — certain to happen and impossible to avoid
"A change was inevitable." (inevitável)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each look-back word with its translation.

Aftermath
Setback
Overlook
Foresee
Reckless
Cautious
Backfire
Bound to
Spare
Pull off
Worthwhile
Inevitable

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Mark looks back at a math test with a bad aftermath. To criticize a choice he did not make, he uses should have plus the past participle: I should have started earlier. To talk about a chance that existed, he uses could have: I could have asked my teacher for help, but it was a setback. To guess about something he is not sure of, he uses might have: my friend never replied, so he might have been busy. And to imagine a different result, he uses would have: I would have passed if I had spared an hour a day. Studying at midnight was reckless and backfired, but it was a worthwhile lesson.

1. Which modal does Mark use to criticize a choice he did not make?
A should have
B will have
C can have
2. How does he guess about something he is not sure of?
A with "should have"
B with "might have"
C with "would have" only
3. What always follows the modal in these structures?
A "of" plus the verb
B "have" plus the past participle
C the present simple

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — modal perfects

GRAMMAR

Para olhar para o passado e julgar ou supor o que era possível, o inglês usa um modal + "have" + particípio passado. / To look back at the past and judge or guess what was possible, English uses a modal + "have" + the past participle.

StructureUse / UsoExample
should have + p.p.criticism / a coisa certa que você não fezI should have studied more.
could have + p.p.possibility / uma chance que existiaI could have taken the earlier train.
might / may have + p.p.guess / um palpite quando não tem certezaShe might have missed the bus.
would have + p.p.imagined result / o resultado imaginadoI would have passed if I had tried.

Key point / Ponto-chave: a forma é sempre modal + have + particípio passado, e todas olham para o PASSADO. Na fala, "should have" soa como should've e "would have" soa como would've — mas escreve-se sempre "have", nunca "of".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I have started earlier."
"We have won the game."
"The plan completely."
"I was too with my time."
"If I spare an hour a day, success is to come."
"In the end, it was a lesson."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of using a modal perfect in the correct order.

?Next, for a chance that existed but did not happen, you use could have.
?First, you decide what you want to say about the past: a criticism, a missed chance, a guess, or an imagined result.
?Finally, for the imagined result of a different choice, you use would have, often with an if clause.
?Then, for the right thing you did not do, you use should have plus the past participle.
?After that, for a guess when you are not sure, you use might have or may have.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I should have started earlier.
Eu deveria ter começado mais cedo.
I could have asked for help.
Eu poderia ter pedido ajuda.
She might have missed the bus.
Ela pode ter perdido o ônibus.
I would have passed if I had tried.
Eu teria passado se tivesse tentado.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct way to look back at each situation.

You forgot to call a friend and regret it. Which is correct?
A I should of called you.
B I should have called you.
C I should called you.
Your friend is late and you are guessing why. Which is most natural?
A He might have missed the bus.
B He should have missed the bus.
C He might have miss the bus.
You did not study, so you did not pass. Imagine the other result.
A I would passed if I had studied.
B I would have passed if I had studied.
C I will have passed if I had studied.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about a real decision from the last few weeks and record a short answer out loud. Say what you should have done differently, what could have happened, and a guess about why it turned out the way it did. Try to use at least three words from this lesson, such as aftermath, setback, reckless or worthwhile.

Survival Card — Lesson 13

1I should have started earlier.Eu deveria ter começado mais cedo.
2I could have asked for help.Eu poderia ter pedido ajuda.
3She might have missed the bus.Ela pode ter perdido o ônibus.
4I would have passed if I had tried.Eu teria passado se tivesse tentado.
5In the end, it was a worthwhile lesson.No fim, foi uma lição que valeu a pena.
Aula 14 — Pre-class

Make It Happen — The Passive Voice

Describe processes and instructions with the passive voice: be + past participle (the order is processed, the files are submitted, the system was monitored, the report was approved by the manager), plus 12 process words: assemble, submit, assign, monitor, verify, trigger, handle, dispose of, streamline, bottleneck, glitch, rollout.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each process word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Assemble — to put parts together to make a complete thing
"The product is assembled by hand." (montar)
Submit — to send something to be checked or approved
"The form is submitted online." (enviar/submeter)
Assign — to give a task or job to a person
"Each step is assigned to a team." (atribuir)
Monitor — to watch and check something over a period of time
"The system is monitored all night." (monitorar)
Verify — to check that something is correct or true
"Every payment is verified before use." (verificar)
Trigger — to cause an action or reaction to start
"An error triggers an alert." (disparar/desencadear)
Handle — to deal with a task, problem or situation
"Complaints are handled quickly." (lidar com)
Dispose of — to get rid of something you no longer need
"Old files are disposed of safely." (descartar)
Streamline — to make a process simpler and more efficient
"The whole flow is streamlined." (otimizar)
Bottleneck — a point in a process where everything slows down
"Testing is the main bottleneck." (gargalo)
Glitch — a small, temporary fault in a machine or system
"A glitch is fixed in seconds." (falha/bug)
Rollout — the launch of a new product, system or update
"The rollout is started slowly." (lançamento)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each process word with its translation.

Assemble
Submit
Assign
Monitor
Verify
Trigger
Handle
Dispose of
Streamline
Bottleneck
Glitch
Rollout

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Mark explains how an app update is built. First, the new code is written and then it is submitted for review. Once it is approved, every feature is inspected, and that stage is the main bottleneck. The rollout is started slowly: a small group is monitored first, and if a glitch is triggered, the release is paused. Old files are disposed of safely. In the past, one update was approved by the lead engineer, and the bugs were handled overnight. Notice that the form is always a form of be plus the past participle, and the tense lives in the verb be.

1. What is the form of the passive voice?
A be + past participle
B have + past participle
C will + base verb
2. Where does the tense (present or past) live?
A in the past participle
B in the verb "be" (is/are or was/were)
C in the word "by"
3. How is the doer added when it matters?
A with "from"
B with "by"
C with "of"

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — the passive voice

GRAMMAR

Para descrever um processo ou instruções, o inglês usa a voz passiva: uma forma do verbo "be" + particípio passado. / To describe a process or instructions, English uses the passive voice: a form of "be" + the past participle.

Tense / TempoStructureExample
Present singular / presenteis + p.p.The order is processed.
Present plural / presenteare + p.p.The files are submitted.
Past singular / passadowas + p.p.The system was monitored.
Past + doer / com o agentewas/were + p.p. + byIt was approved by the manager.

Key point / Ponto-chave: o tempo verbal fica no be (is/are = presente, was/were = passado), e o verbo principal vira sempre o particípio passado. O agente (quem faz) entra no fim com by, mas só quando importa — num processo, ele costuma ser omitido porque a ação importa mais que a pessoa.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The order processed in minutes."
"The files submitted online."
"The system monitored all night."
"The report was approved the manager."
"Every payment is carefully before use."
"The old files are of safely."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of building a passive sentence in the correct order.

?After that, you add the past participle of the main verb, like processed, assembled or handled.
?First, you decide what the result of the action is, because in the passive the result comes first.
?Finally, if the doer is obvious or general, you simply leave it out.
?Then, you choose the correct form of be: is or are for the present, was or were for the past.
?Next, if the doer is important, you add it at the end with the word by.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

The order is processed in minutes.
O pedido é processado em minutos.
The files are submitted online.
Os arquivos são enviados online.
The system was monitored all night.
O sistema foi monitorado a noite toda.
The report was approved by the manager.
O relatório foi aprovado pelo gerente.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct passive sentence for each situation.

You want to say that orders are dealt with fast, without naming who does it. Which is correct?
A Orders processed quickly.
B Orders are processed quickly.
C Orders is processed quickly.
You want to describe a past action and name the doer. Which is most natural?
A The bug was fixed by the developer.
B The bug was fixed from the developer.
C The bug fixed by the developer.
You describe a present process with a plural subject. Which is correct?
A All parts is inspected first.
B All parts are inspected first.
C All parts are inspect first.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about a product, app or game you use, and record a short answer out loud explaining how it is made, updated or delivered. Use the passive voice (be + past participle) for at least three steps, and try to use process words such as assemble, submit, monitor, verify or streamline.

Survival Card — Lesson 14

1The order is processed in minutes.O pedido é processado em minutos.
2The files are submitted online.Os arquivos são enviados online.
3The system was monitored all night.O sistema foi monitorado a noite toda.
4The report was approved by the manager.O relatório foi aprovado pelo gerente.
5The whole process is streamlined.O processo todo é otimizado.
Aula 15 — Pre-class

The People Who Make It Work — Relative Clauses

Connect ideas about people and things with relative clauses: who (people), which (things), that (both, defining only), where (places), whose (possession); defining clauses take no commas, non-defining clauses always take commas. Plus 12 people words: mentor, colleague, expert, founder, pioneer, contributor, supervisor, intern, specialist, advisor, recruit, peer.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each people word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Mentor — an experienced person who guides and advises a less experienced one
"The mentor who trained me is retired now." (mentor/orientador)
Colleague — a person you work with in the same place
"My colleague, who sits next to me, is helpful." (colega de trabalho)
Expert — a person who has deep knowledge or skill in something
"She is the expert that designed the system." (especialista/perito)
Founder — a person who creates and starts a company or project
"The founder whose idea changed everything is shy." (fundador/a)
Pioneer — a person who is among the first to do or explore something
"He is a pioneer whose work inspired many." (pioneiro/a)
Contributor — a person who adds work, ideas or money to a project
"The contributors who fix bugs work at night." (colaborador/a)
Supervisor — a person who checks and manages the work of others
"The supervisor who reviews my work is fair." (supervisor/a)
Intern — a young person who works to learn and gain experience
"The intern who joined in June is talented." (estagiário/a)
Specialist — a person who focuses deeply on one specific area
"The specialist who handles security is busy." (especialista)
Advisor — a person who gives advice and guidance to others
"The advisor whose tips I trust is wise." (conselheiro/a)
Recruit — a new member who has just joined a team or group
"The recruits who started today look nervous." (recruta/novato/a)
Peer — a person who is at the same level, age or status as you
"My peers, who study with me, share notes." (colega/par/igual)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each people word with its translation.

Mentor
Colleague
Expert
Founder
Pioneer
Contributor
Supervisor
Intern
Specialist
Advisor
Recruit
Peer

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Mark describes the people behind his favorite game. The founder, who failed twice first, started the studio. The expert that shaped the style is Theo, the one who joined ten years ago. There is a team of contributors who fix bugs at night, in an office where they almost never stop working. His favorite is the community manager, who reads thousands of messages a week. The players whose ideas are the best often see them become features. Notice the two kinds: a clause with no commas tells us which person we mean (defining), and a clause between commas just adds extra information (non-defining) and never uses "that".

1. Which relative pronoun is used for people?
A who
B which
C where
2. What tells us a clause is non-defining (extra information)?
A it has no commas
B it is placed between commas
C it always uses "that"
3. Which pronoun shows possession?
A where
B whose
C which

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — relative clauses

GRAMMAR

Para adicionar informação a um substantivo, o inglês usa uma relação (relative clause) com who, which, that, where ou whose. / To add information to a noun, English uses a relative clause with who, which, that, where or whose.

Pronoun / PronomeUsed forExample
who / quem, quepeople / pessoasThe expert who built it.
which / que, o qualthings / coisasThe app which she made.
where / ondeplaces / lugaresThe office where they work.
whose / cujo, cujapossession / posseThe founder whose idea won.

Key point / Ponto-chave: uma defining clause diz exatamente qual pessoa ou coisa e não usa vírgula (pode usar "that" e até omitir o pronome se for objeto). Uma non-defining clause adiciona informação extra (removível) e sempre usa vírgulas — e nela você nunca usa "that".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The mentor guided me changed my life."
"The app she built is huge."
"The office they work never closes."
"The founder idea failed never gave up."
"My colleague, who lives nearby, is a great ."
"The who fix bugs work at night."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of building a relative clause in the correct order.

?After that, you decide if the clause is defining or non-defining.
?First, you find the noun you want to describe, like the mentor or the studio.
?Finally, if the clause only adds extra information, you put it between commas.
?Then, you choose the right relative pronoun: who, which, where or whose.
?Next, if the clause is essential, you add no commas.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

The mentor who guided me changed my life.
O mentor que me orientou mudou a minha vida.
The app which she built is huge.
O aplicativo que ela construiu é enorme.
My colleague, who lives in Lisbon, joined us.
Meu colega, que mora em Lisboa, entrou no time.
The founder whose idea failed never gave up.
O fundador cuja ideia falhou nunca desistiu.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct sentence with a relative clause for each situation.

You want to say exactly which person trained you (essential, no extra info). Which is correct?
A The mentor, who trained me, is wise.
B The mentor who trained me is wise.
C The mentor which trained me is wise.
You add extra, removable information about one colleague. Which is most natural?
A My colleague, who lives nearby, helped me.
B My colleague that lives nearby helped me, with commas.
C My colleague, that lives nearby, helped me.
You want to describe the place where the team works. Which is correct?
A The office which they work is small.
B The office where they work is small.
C The office whose they work is small.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about the people behind something you love — a game, an app or a band — and record a short answer out loud describing them. Use relative clauses (who, which, where, whose) for at least three of them, and try to use people words such as founder, mentor, expert or contributor. Mix one defining clause (no commas) and one non-defining clause (with commas).

Survival Card — Lesson 15

1The mentor who guided me changed my life.O mentor que me orientou mudou a minha vida.
2The app which she built is huge.O aplicativo que ela construiu é enorme.
3My colleague, who lives in Lisbon, joined us.Meu colega, que mora em Lisboa, entrou no time.
4The office where they work never closes.O escritório onde eles trabalham nunca fecha.
5The founder whose idea failed never gave up.O fundador cuja ideia falhou nunca desistiu.
Aula 16 — Pre-class

Said and Done — Reported Questions

Retell the questions people ask with reported questions: ask + if/whether for yes/no questions, ask + a wh-word for wh-questions, statement order, no do/does/did, the verb shifts one step back and there is no question mark (she asked where I lived, he asked if I was ready), plus 12 words for asking and answering: inquire, query, wonder, raise, probe, look into, respond, reply, mention, point out, request, refer to.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Inquire — to ask for information in a formal or polite way
"She inquired whether the room was free." (indagar)
Query — a question, especially one asking for clarification
"He raised a query about the price." (dúvida)
Wonder — to ask yourself something because you are curious
"She wondered what the answer was." (perguntar-se)
Raise — to bring up a question, point or topic to discuss
"A fan raised a question about a sequel." (levantar)
Probe — to ask searching questions to learn more
"She probed why they made that choice." (sondar)
Look into — to investigate or examine something closely
"They promised to look into the issue." (investigar)
Respond — to say or do something in answer to a question
"He responded that he was not sure." (responder)
Reply — to give an answer back to someone
"She replied that she would think about it." (replicar)
Mention — to say something briefly, without full detail
"He mentioned that the launch was delayed." (mencionar)
Point out — to make someone notice a fact or detail
"She pointed out that testing took months." (apontar)
Request — to ask for something in a polite, formal way
"The host requested that he reveal a secret." (solicitar)
Refer to — to mention or speak about something
"She referred to his earlier answer." (referir-se a)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Inquire
Query
Wonder
Raise
Probe
Look into
Respond
Reply
Mention
Point out
Request
Refer to

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Mark retells an interview with a game designer. First, the host asked how the studio had started, and the designer explained it. Then she inquired whether the team had expected the success, and he replied that they were stunned. She wondered what the hardest part was, and he pointed out that testing had taken months. A fan asked if there would be a sequel, but the designer responded that nothing was confirmed. Notice that each reported question uses statement order (subject before verb), drops do, does or did, shifts the verb one step back, and has no question mark.

1. What word do we use to report a yes/no question?
A that
B if or whether
C what
2. What happens to do/does/did in a reported question?
A it is dropped
B it is kept at the start
C it moves to the end
3. Does a reported question take a question mark?
A yes, always
B no, it is really a statement
C only for wh-questions

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — reported questions

GRAMMAR

Para recontar uma pergunta, o inglês usa a reported question: o verbo de relato (ask, wonder, inquire) + if/whether (para sim/não) ou a palavra interrogativa (para wh-), em ordem de afirmação. / To retell a question, English uses a reported question: the reporting verb (ask, wonder, inquire) + if/whether (for yes/no) or the wh-word (for wh-questions), in statement order.

Type / TipoDirectReported
Yes/No"Are you ready?"She asked if I was ready.
Yes/No (formal)"Did they win?"She asked whether they had won.
Wh-"Where do you live?"She asked where I lived.
Wh-"What does he want?"She asked what he wanted.

Key point / Ponto-chave: a reported question usa ordem de afirmação (sujeito antes do verbo), não usa do/does/did, o verbo costuma recuar um tempo (live → lived) e não leva ponto de interrogação. Use if/whether para perguntas de sim/não e a palavra interrogativa (where, what, why) para wh-questions.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"She asked I lived."
"He asked I was ready."
"She wondered what he ."
"They inquired we had won."
"He out that nothing was confirmed."
"The host whether the team had expected the success."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of building a reported question in the correct order.

?After that, you put the subject and the verb in normal statement order, with no inversion.
?First, you start with a reporting verb, like she asked, he wondered or they inquired.
?Finally, you shift the verb one step back and never add a question mark.
?Then, for a yes/no question you add if or whether, and for a wh-question you keep the question word.
?Next, you remove the auxiliary do, does or did, because it is not a real question.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

She asked where I lived.
Ela perguntou onde eu morava.
He asked if I was ready.
Ele perguntou se eu estava pronto.
She wondered what he wanted.
Ela se perguntou o que ele queria.
They inquired whether we had won.
Eles indagaram se tínhamos vencido.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct reported question for each situation.

You want to report the question "Where do you work?" Which is correct?
A She asked where do I work.
B She asked where I worked.
C She asked where did I work?
You report a yes/no question, formally. Which is most natural?
A He inquired whether the deal was final.
B He inquired that the deal was final.
C He inquired was the deal final?
You report "What does he want?" Which is correct?
A She wondered what does he want.
B She wondered what did he want.
C She wondered what he wanted.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about the last interview, podcast or chat you watched, and record a short answer out loud retelling the questions that were asked. Use reported questions (she asked if..., he wondered what...) for at least three of them, and try to use asking words such as inquire, probe, wonder or reply.

Survival Card — Lesson 16

1She asked where I lived.Ela perguntou onde eu morava.
2He asked if I was ready.Ele perguntou se eu estava pronto.
3She wondered what he wanted.Ela se perguntou o que ele queria.
4They inquired whether we had won.Eles indagaram se tínhamos vencido.
5He pointed out that nothing was confirmed.Ele apontou que nada estava confirmado.
Aula 17 — Pre-class

To Play or Playing? — Gerund vs Infinitive

Talk about your preferences with gerund and infinitive: use the gerund (verb + -ing) after liking and disliking verbs (enjoy, avoid, fancy, cannot stand, mind, dread) and after a preposition (look forward to seeing), and the infinitive (to + verb) after wanting and planning verbs (want, hope, decide, manage, afford, tend), plus 12 words for likes and plans: avoid, mind, fancy, cannot stand, look forward to, give up, keep on, manage, afford, tend, bother, dread.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Avoid — to keep yourself from doing something (+ -ing)
"I avoid leaving things to the last minute." (evitar)
Mind — to feel annoyed or bothered by something (+ -ing)
"I do not mind practicing every day." (importar-se)
Fancy — to want or feel like doing something (+ -ing)
"I fancy learning something competitive." (ter vontade de)
Cannot stand — to hate or strongly dislike something (+ -ing)
"I cannot stand waiting in long lines." (não suportar)
Look forward to — to feel excited about something coming (+ -ing)
"I look forward to joining the chess club." (ansiar por)
Give up — to stop doing something completely (+ -ing)
"I will never give up learning the guitar." (desistir)
Keep on — to continue doing something (+ -ing)
"She keeps on practicing every evening." (continuar)
Manage — to succeed in doing something hard (+ to + verb)
"I managed to build a real routine." (conseguir)
Afford — to have enough money or time for something (+ to + verb)
"I cannot afford to miss a single class." (ter condições de)
Tend — to usually do something; to have a habit (+ to + verb)
"I tend to do my homework right away." (costumar)
Bother — to make the effort to do something (+ -ing or to + verb)
"I do not bother checking my phone in class." (dar-se ao trabalho)
Dread — to feel great fear about something ahead (+ -ing)
"I dread missing a single session." (temer)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Avoid
Mind
Fancy
Cannot stand
Look forward to
Give up
Keep on
Manage
Afford
Tend
Bother
Dread

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Mark talks about his free time. He really enjoys playing the guitar and does not mind practicing for an hour a day. He cannot stand waiting in long lines, so he always plans to arrive early. This year he has decided to learn chess, because he fancies learning something competitive, and he is looking forward to joining the club. He tends to do his homework right after school and avoids leaving things to the weekend. Notice that liking and disliking verbs take the gerund (-ing), planning verbs take the infinitive (to + verb), and after a preposition we always use -ing.

1. Which form do we use after enjoy and cannot stand?
A to + verb
B the gerund (-ing)
C the base verb
2. Which form do we use after decide and plan?
A the infinitive (to + verb)
B the gerund (-ing)
C the past form
3. After a preposition (like "to" in look forward to), which form?
A to + verb
B always the gerund (-ing)
C the base verb

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — gerund vs infinitive

GRAMMAR

Depois de certos verbos, o inglês usa o gerund (verbo + -ing) e, depois de outros, o infinitive (to + verbo base). Verbos de gostar/não gostar pedem -ing; verbos de querer/planejar pedem to + verbo; depois de preposição, sempre -ing. / After certain verbs English uses the gerund (verb + -ing) and after others the infinitive (to + base verb). Liking/disliking verbs take -ing; wanting/planning verbs take to + verb; after a preposition, always -ing.

Form / FormaAfter verbs likeExample
-ing (gerund)enjoy, avoid, fancy, can't stand, mind, dreadI enjoy playing.
to + verbwant, hope, decide, manage, afford, tendI decided to study.
eitherstart, love, prefer, beginI love reading / to read.
-ing (after preposition)look forward to, give up, keep onI look forward to seeing you.

Key point / Ponto-chave: verbos de gostar/não gostar usam o gerund (-ing); verbos de querer/planejar usam o infinitive (to + verbo); alguns aceitam as duas formas com quase o mesmo sentido; depois de preposição, sempre -ing (I look forward to seeing you, não "to see").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I enjoy the guitar."
"She decided chess."
"I avoid things to the last minute."
"I look forward to you."
"We managed on time."
"I cannot stand in long lines."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of choosing gerund or infinitive in the correct order.

?After that, if the verb is one of liking or disliking, you use the gerund, the verb plus -ing.
?First, you choose your main verb, such as enjoy, want, decide or avoid.
?Finally, remember that after a preposition you always use the gerund, as in look forward to seeing.
?Then, you check which form that verb prefers, because some take the gerund and others the infinitive.
?Next, if the verb is one of wanting or planning, you use the infinitive, to plus the base verb.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I enjoy playing the guitar.
Eu gosto de tocar violão.
I have decided to learn chess.
Eu decidi aprender xadrez.
I cannot stand waiting in lines.
Eu não suporto esperar em filas.
I look forward to seeing you.
Estou ansioso para te ver.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct sentence for each situation.

You want to say you like a hobby. Which is correct?
A I enjoy to read comics.
B I enjoy reading comics.
C I enjoy read comics.
You want to say you made a plan. Which is most natural?
A I have decided to join the club.
B I have decided joining the club.
C I have decided join the club.
You want to express excitement about the future. Which is correct?
A I look forward to see you.
B I look forward see you.
C I look forward to seeing you.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Talk about your free time and record a short answer out loud. Say three things you enjoy or cannot stand doing (gerund: -ing) and two things you have decided or plan to do (infinitive: to + verb). Try to use verbs such as avoid, fancy, manage and look forward to.

Survival Card — Lesson 17

1I enjoy playing the guitar.Eu gosto de tocar violão.
2I have decided to learn chess.Eu decidi aprender xadrez.
3I cannot stand waiting in lines.Eu não suporto esperar em filas.
4I look forward to seeing you.Estou ansioso para te ver.
5I managed to build a routine.Eu consegui criar uma rotina.
Aula 18 — Pre-class

Looking Ahead — Future Continuous & Future Perfect

Talk about the future with two new forms: use the future continuous (will be + verb + -ing) for an action in progress at a future moment (this time next year I will be studying abroad), and the future perfect (will have + past participle) for an action finished before a future moment (by next June I will have finished my course), plus 12 words for goals, progress and time: predict, achieve, milestone, deadline, accomplish, upcoming, eventually, gradually, in progress, complete, set a goal, anticipate.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Predict — to say what you think will happen
"I predict I will be living abroad in five years." (prever)
Achieve — to succeed in getting something you worked for
"By June I will have achieved my main goal." (alcançar)
Milestone — an important moment that marks real progress
"Finishing this course will be a big milestone." (marco)
Deadline — the final time or date to finish a task
"By the deadline I will have finished the project." (prazo)
Accomplish — to finish something important successfully
"I will have accomplished a lot by the end of the year." (realizar)
Upcoming — happening soon; coming in the near future
"I am getting ready for the upcoming exams." (próximo)
Eventually — in the end, after some time has passed
"Eventually I will have learned to play really well." (no fim)
Gradually — slowly and steadily, little by little
"I will be improving gradually every week." (gradualmente)
In progress — still happening and not finished yet
"This time tomorrow my project will still be in progress." (em andamento)
Complete — fully finished, with nothing left to do
"By Friday my homework will be complete." (completo)
Set a goal — to decide on an aim and plan to reach it
"I set a goal to read one book every month." (definir uma meta)
Anticipate — to expect something and prepare for it
"I anticipate I will be travelling a lot next year." (prever/antecipar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Predict
Achieve
Milestone
Deadline
Accomplish
Upcoming
Eventually
Gradually
In progress
Complete
Set a goal
Anticipate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Mark is looking ahead. He has set a goal for the year, and he predicts a busy time. This time next month, he will be studying hard for his upcoming exams, and his school project will still be in progress. But by the deadline in June, he will have finished the project and he will have achieved a real milestone. Notice the two forms: will be + verb + -ing pictures an action in progress at a future moment, while will have + past participle pictures an action that is already finished before that moment.

1. Which form pictures an action in progress at a future moment?
A will have + past participle
B will be + verb + -ing
C the base verb
2. Which form pictures an action finished before a future moment?
A will have + past participle
B will be + verb + -ing
C the past simple
3. Which time marker usually points to the future perfect?
A right now
B by next June
C yesterday

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — future continuous & future perfect

GRAMMAR

O future continuous (will be + verbo + -ing) descreve uma ação em andamento num momento futuro (this time next year I will be studying). O future perfect (will have + particcipio passado) descreve uma ação já concluída antes de um momento futuro (by next June I will have finished). / The future continuous (will be + verb + -ing) describes an action in progress at a future moment; the future perfect (will have + past participle) describes an action already finished before a future moment.

Form / FormaUse it forExample
will be + verb-ingan action in progress at a future momentThis time tomorrow I will be flying.
will have + past participlean action finished before a future momentBy June I will have finished.
Time markers (continuous)this time tomorrow, at eight tonightAt six I will be studying.
Time markers (perfect)by next year, by the deadlineBy next year I will have moved.

Key point / Ponto-chave: will be combina com -ing (ação em andamento); will have combina com o particcipio passado (ação concluída). Use by + tempo com o future perfect e this time + tempo com o future continuous.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"This time next year I abroad."
"By next June I my course."
"At eight tonight I the guitar."
"By the deadline we the project."
"This time tomorrow they to the coast."
"By next month I my goal."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of choosing the future form in the correct order.

?Next, if the action is finished before that time, you use the future perfect, will have plus the past participle.
?First, you decide whether the action is in progress or already finished at the future moment.
?Finally, you check that will be matches an -ing verb and will have matches a past participle.
?Then, if the action is in progress at that time, you use the future continuous, will be plus verb plus -ing.
?After that, you add a time marker such as this time tomorrow or by next year to make the moment clear.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

This time next year I will be studying abroad.
Daqui a um ano estarei estudando no exterior.
By next June I will have finished my course.
Até junho eu terei terminado meu curso.
By the deadline I will have completed it.
Até o prazo eu terei concluído isso.
By then I will have achieved a real milestone.
Até lá eu terei alcançado um marco de verdade.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct sentence for each situation.

You want to picture an action in progress at a future time. Which is correct?
A At six I will be study.
B At six I will be studying.
C At six I will have studying.
You want to say an action will be finished before a future time. Which is most natural?
A By Friday I will have finished it.
B By Friday I will have finishing it.
C By Friday I will finished it.
Which sentence correctly uses a future-perfect time marker?
A Right now I will have left.
B Yesterday I will have left.
C By next year I will have left.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Talk about your year ahead and record a short answer out loud. Say three things you will be doing at a future moment (future continuous: will be + -ing) and two things you will have finished before then (future perfect: will have + past participle). Try to use words such as set a goal, milestone, eventually and accomplish.

Survival Card — Lesson 18

1This time next year I will be studying abroad.Daqui a um ano estarei estudando no exterior.
2By next June I will have finished my course.Até junho eu terei terminado meu curso.
3At eight tonight I will be practicing the guitar.Às oito da noite estarei praticando violão.
4By the deadline I will have completed it.Até o prazo eu terei concluído isso.
5By then I will have achieved a real milestone.Até lá eu terei alcançado um marco de verdade.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Caught in the Moment -- Participle Clauses

Describe two actions in one smooth sentence with participle clauses (Sitting in the row, I watched / Stunned by the goal, I froze). New vocabulary: glance, startle, sprint, clutch, gasp, scramble, drenched, exhausted, thrilled, stunned, overwhelmed, collapse.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Glance -- to take a very quick look at something
"He glanced at the clock and saw he was late." (Dar uma olhada rápida)
Startle -- to surprise someone suddenly, making them jump
"The loud horn startled the whole crowd." (Assustar)
Sprint -- to run at full speed for a short distance
"She sprinted to catch the last train." (Disparar / correr)
Clutch -- to hold something tightly
"He clutched the ball before the shot." (Agarrar com força)
Gasp -- to take a sudden, sharp breath because of shock
"The crowd gasped when the player fell." (Ofegar de surpresa)
Scramble -- to move quickly in a hurried, clumsy way
"She scrambled to find her ticket." (Atrapalhar-se / correr atabalhoado)
Drenched -- completely wet, soaked through
"Drenched in sweat, the player scored." (Encharcado)
Exhausted -- extremely tired, with no energy left
"Exhausted after the match, he fell asleep." (Exausto)
Thrilled -- extremely happy and excited
"Thrilled by the win, the fans sang all night." (Empolgadíssimo)
Stunned -- so shocked you cannot react for a moment
"Stunned by the goal, I could not move." (Atordoado / pasmo)
Overwhelmed -- feeling a very strong emotion that is hard to control
"Overwhelmed by the noise, she cried with joy." (Tomado pela emoção)
Collapse -- to fall down suddenly from weakness or shock
"Exhausted, the runner collapsed at the finish line." (Desabar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Glance
Startle
Sprint
Clutch
Gasp
Scramble
Drenched
Exhausted
Thrilled
Stunned
Overwhelmed
Collapse

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Sitting in the front row, Mark watched the cup final. In the last minute, the striker got the ball. Sprinting down the wing, he beat two defenders. Clutching the ball, he stayed calm and looked up. Drenched in sweat and completely exhausted, he scored the winning goal. Stunned by what he had just seen, Mark could not move for a moment. Then, overwhelmed by the noise, he jumped up and screamed with everyone else.

1. Where was Mark when the goal was scored?
A Sitting in the front row.
B Running on the pitch.
C Watching at home on TV.
2. What did the striker do before he scored?
A He sat down and waited.
B Sprinting down the wing, he beat two defenders and clutched the ball.
C He passed the ball back to the keeper.
3. How did Mark react after the goal?
A Stunned at first, then overwhelmed, he jumped up and screamed.
B He left the stadium immediately.
C He felt nothing at all.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Participle Clauses

GRAMMAR

Participle clauses join two ideas with the same subject into one sentence. Use the -ing form for an active action at the same time, and the past participle for a state or feeling. / Participle clauses unem duas ideias com o mesmo sujeito numa frase só. Use a forma -ing para uma ação ativa ao mesmo tempo, e o past participle para um estado ou sentimento.

TypeFormExample
Present participleverb + -ing (active)"Sitting in the row, I watched the game."
Past participlepast participle (state)"Drenched in sweat, he scored."
Reasonreplaces "because""Stunned by the goal, I froze."
Sequencefirst action + -ing"Grabbing my coat, I ran out."

Key point / Ponto-chave: o sujeito do participle clause e o da frase principal devem ser a mesma pessoa. Sempre use uma vírgula entre os dois. The subject of the clause must match the main subject; always use a comma.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct participle or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

" in the front row, I had a great view."
" down the wing, he beat two defenders."
" in sweat, he scored the goal."
" by the goal, I could not move."
" by the noise, she cried with joy."
" for my phone, I dropped my drink."

Stage 2: Build a Participle Clause in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of building a participle clause in the correct order.

?You change that verb into the -ing form to make a present participle.
?You take the two sentences that share the same subject.
?You read the new sentence aloud to check that it sounds natural.
?You find the action that happens at the same time.
?You put the participle clause at the front and join it with a comma.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Sitting in the front row, I had a great view.
Sentado na primeira fila, eu tinha uma ótima visão.
Sprinting down the wing, he beat two defenders.
Disparando pela lateral, ele passou por dois zagueiros.
Stunned by the goal, I could not move for a second.
Atordoado com o gol, não consegui me mexer por um segundo.
Overwhelmed by the noise, I jumped up and screamed.
Tomado pela emoção com o barulho, eu pulei e gritei.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to say you watched the game while you were in the front row. Which is best?
A "Sit in the front row, I watched the game."
B "Sitting in the front row, I watched the game."
C "Sitting in the front row, the game was watched."
You were too shocked to move after the goal. Which is best?
A "Stunned by the goal, I could not move."
B "Stunning by the goal, I could not move."
C "Stunned by the goal the player could not move."
Which sentence has the WRONG subject?
A "Running to the gate, the train left without me."
B "Running to the gate, I missed the train."
C "Clutching the ticket, I ran to the gate."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Tell the story of an exciting moment you lived -- a game, a concert, or a surprise. Use at least three participle clauses (Sitting..., Sprinting..., Stunned by..., Overwhelmed by...) and new words like thrilled, drenched, exhausted, and scramble.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1Sitting in the front row, I had a great view.Sentado na primeira fila, eu tinha uma ótima visão.
2Sprinting down the wing, he beat two defenders.Disparando pela lateral, ele passou por dois zagueiros.
3Drenched in sweat, he scored the winning goal.Encharcado de suor, ele fez o gol da vitória.
4Stunned by the goal, I could not move for a second.Atordoado com o gol, não consegui me mexer por um segundo.
5Overwhelmed by the noise, I jumped up and screamed.Tomado pela emoção com o barulho, eu pulei e gritei.
Aula 20 — Pre-class

The Power of Storytelling — The Past Perfect

Tell longer, richer stories. Use the past perfect (had + past participle) for the EARLIER of two past actions and the past simple for the later one (by the time I arrived, the train had already left), plus 12 storyteller's words: narrator, plot, twist, suspense, flashback, unfold, gripping, climax, meanwhile, out of the blue, turning point, to cut a long story short.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Narrator — the person who tells a story
"As the narrator, I will start at the beginning." (narrador)
Plot — the main events of a story, in order
"The plot of the film was easy to follow." (enredo)
Twist — a surprising, unexpected turn in a story
"The ending had a twist I did not expect." (reviravolta)
Suspense — an exciting, nervous feeling about what happens next
"The suspense kept me reading until midnight." (suspense)
Flashback — a scene that goes back to an earlier time
"A flashback showed what had happened before." (cena retrospectiva)
Unfold — to slowly develop or be revealed, step by step
"We watched the story unfold scene by scene." (se desenrolar)
Gripping — so exciting that you cannot stop watching or reading
"It was a gripping story from start to finish." (envolvente)
Climax — the most exciting, most important point of a story
"At the climax, the hero finally won." (clímax)
Meanwhile — at the same time, while something else is happening
"Meanwhile, my brother stayed completely calm." (enquanto isso)
Out of the blue — suddenly and unexpectedly, with no warning
"Out of the blue, it started to rain." (do nada)
Turning point — the moment when the situation changes completely
"That was the turning point of the whole trip." (ponto de virada)
To cut a long story short — a phrase used to skip details and reach the end quickly
"To cut a long story short, we got home safely." (resumindo)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Narrator
Plot
Twist
Suspense
Flashback
Unfold
Gripping
Climax
Meanwhile
Out of the blue
Turning point
To cut a long story short

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Mark loves a good story. By the time he got home from school, his family had already started the film. He had missed the opening, so at first the plot was confusing. Meanwhile, a flashback explained what had happened before, and the story began to unfold. Then, out of the blue, there was a twist at the climax. Notice the pattern: the past perfect (had + past participle) shows the action that happened FIRST, while the past simple shows the action that came next.

1. Which form shows the action that happened FIRST?
A the past simple
B the past perfect (had + past participle)
C the present perfect
2. In "by the time he got home, his family had started the film", which happened first?
A the film started
B he got home
C both at the same time
3. Which phrase means "suddenly, with no warning"?
A to cut a long story short
B out of the blue
C meanwhile

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — the past perfect for stories

GRAMMAR

Numa história, duas ações passadas nem sempre acontecem na ordem em que você as conta. O past perfect (had + particcipio passado) mostra a ação que aconteceu PRIMEIRO, e o past simple mostra a que veio depois (by the time I arrived, the train had already left). / In a story, two past actions do not always happen in the order you tell them. The past perfect (had + past participle) marks the EARLIER action; the past simple marks the later one.

Form / FormaUse it forExample
had + past participlethe EARLIER of two past actionsThe train had left.
past simplethe LATER past actionI arrived late.
Negativehad not (hadn't) + past participleWe had not brought a map.
Time wordsby the time, when, before, after, alreadyBy the time I called, she had gone.

Key point / Ponto-chave: após had, use sempre o particcipio passado (had gone, had seen), nunca o verbo base nem o -ing. O past perfect é a "máquina do tempo" da história: diz qual ação veio primeiro.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"By the time I arrived, the train ."
"She was sad because she the match."
"When we got home, the storm ."
"He could not get in because he his key."
"The streets were wet because it all night."
"By the time the hero arrived, the villain ."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of building a past-perfect sentence in the correct order.

?Next, you put the earlier event in the past perfect, had plus the past participle.
?First, you choose the two past events you want to connect in your story.
?Finally, you add a time word such as by the time or when to make the order clear.
?Then, you decide which event happened earlier.
?After that, you put the later event in the past simple.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

By the time I arrived, the train had already left.
Quando eu cheguei, o trem já tinha partido.
Out of the blue, everything changed.
Do nada, tudo mudou.
That was the real turning point.
Aquele foi o verdadeiro ponto de virada.
And the twist was that no one had expected it.
E a reviravolta foi que ninguém tinha esperado por isso.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the correct sentence for each situation.

You want to show that one past action happened before another. Which is correct?
A By the time we arrived, the show had start.
B By the time we arrived, the show had started.
C By the time we arrived, the show had starting.
You want to give the reason in the past. Which is most natural?
A He was tired because he had run a lot.
B He was tired because he have run a lot.
C He was tired because he had running a lot.
Which sentence uses a story word correctly?
A The turning point was very boring and slow.
B The twist at the climax surprised everyone.
C The plot is the person who tells the story.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Tell a short story about a memorable day and record it out loud. Use at least three past-perfect sentences (had + past participle) to show what had happened before, and try to use words such as out of the blue, turning point, meanwhile and twist.

Survival Card — Lesson 20

1By the time I arrived, the train had already left.Quando eu cheguei, o trem já tinha partido.
2Out of the blue, everything changed.Do nada, tudo mudou.
3That was the real turning point.Aquele foi o verdadeiro ponto de virada.
4To cut a long story short, it all worked out.Resumindo, tudo deu certo.
5And the twist was that no one had expected it.E a reviravolta foi que ninguém tinha esperado por isso.

Aulas Disponíveis

01
Beyond the Screen — Who I Really Am
Identity & Self-Expression — 30 slides
02
What Makes Me Tick
Personality & Mixed Tenses — 30 slides
03
The People Around Me
Relative Clauses — 30 slides
04
Online vs Offline
Passive Voice — 30 slides
05
What Would I Do?
Second Conditional — 26 slides
06
He Said, She Said
Reported Speech: news reporting — 28 slides
07
Connecting Ideas
Discourse markers for structured opinions — 28 slides
08
Getting Things Done
Causative have/get — describing services — 28 slides
09
Bring It All Together
Mini-presentation — signposting & delivery — 28 slides
10
Take the Stage
Phase 1 Showcase — full presentation & Q&A — 28 slides
11
Back in the Day
Phase 2 opener — childhood memories with used to & would — 28 slides
12
I Wish Things Were Different
Phase 2 — wish & if only for regrets and desires — 28 slides
13
What Could Have Happened
Phase 2 — modal perfects (should have, could have, might have) for past speculation — 28 slides
14
Make It Happen
Phase 2 — the passive voice in everyday processes and instructions — 28 slides
15
The People Who Make It Work
Phase 2 — relative clauses (defining and non-defining) to describe people and things — 28 slides
16
Said and Done
Phase 2 — reported questions, for retelling what people asked — 28 slides
17
To Play or Playing?
Phase 2 — gerund vs infinitive, for talking about your preferences — 28 slides
18
Looking Ahead
Phase 2 — future continuous & future perfect, for talking about what you will be doing — 28 slides
19
Caught in the Moment -- Participle Clauses
Participle clauses (-ing / past participle), for telling vivid stories — 28 slides
20
The Power of Storytelling
Phase 2 — the past perfect for sequencing a story (had + past participle), for telling longer, richer stories — 28 slides

Complementary Materials — Lesson 1

Lesson 1 — Beyond the Screen — Who I Really Am

Series
Stranger Things — Season 1, Episode 1

A group of friends deal with identity, growing up, and standing out. Great vocabulary for talking about yourself and your world.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and notice how the characters describe each other.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
6 Minute English — BBC: "What Makes You, You?"

A short podcast about identity and personality. Perfect level for B2 listening practice.

Tip: listen once without pausing, then listen again and write down 3 new expressions.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
TED-Ed: "What Makes You Special?"

A short animated video about what makes each person unique. Uses vocabulary from this lesson like "stand out" and "perspective."

Tip: after watching, try to answer the question in English using your own perspective.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — What Makes Me Tick

Series
The Good Place — Season 1, Episode 1

A comedy about personality, moral character, and what makes us who we are. Explores traits like ambition and empathy.

Tip: notice how characters describe each other's personality traits.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
6 Minute English — BBC: "Are You a Perfectionist?"

Explores driven and ambitious personality types. Great listening practice at B2 level.

Tip: write down 3 personality adjectives you hear and use them in a sentence.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
AsapSCIENCE: "Are You An Introvert or Extrovert?"

A fun video about personality science. Uses vocabulary like traits, empathetic, and personality types.

Tip: take the quiz in the video and describe your results using this lesson's vocabulary.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — The People Around Me

Series
Modern Family — Season 1, Episode 1

A comedy about different types of families and relationships. Perfect for vocabulary about bonds, siblings, and close-knit families.

Tip: pay attention to how family members describe their relationships with each other.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
6 Minute English — BBC: "What Makes a Good Friend?"

Discusses the qualities of friendship, counting on people, and strong bonds.

Tip: listen for relative clauses and write down any examples you hear.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
TED Talk: "The Secret to Making Friends as an Adult"

Explores how we build bonds and maintain relationships. Great for practicing relative clauses in context.

Tip: describe one of your relationships using 3 relative clauses after watching.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Online vs Offline

Series
Black Mirror — Season 3, Episode 1: "Nosedive"

A dystopian story about social media ratings and perception. Explores themes of authenticity, filters, and digital footprints.

Tip: notice passive voice structures. Can you find 5 examples?

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
6 Minute English — BBC: "Is Social Media Making Us Sad?"

Discusses screen time, algorithms, and how social media affects our perception of happiness.

Tip: summarize the main argument using passive voice.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Kurzgesagt: "Social Media Is Designed to Be Addictive"

An animated explainer about how apps are designed to maximize screen time using algorithms and curated content.

Tip: after watching, write 5 sentences about the video using passive voice.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — He Said, She Said — Reported Speech

Series
Ted Lasso — Season 1, Episode 1

A sports comedy full of reporters, press conferences, and rumours about a football club. Perfect for hearing reported speech ("He said that...", "The coach claimed...") in a fun context.

Tip: every time a reporter talks about a player, write down what they said using "He/She said that...".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
6 Minute English — BBC: "Fake news and rumours"

A short, clear podcast about how news spreads and how people claim, deny, and exaggerate stories. Great B2 listening that reuses today's vocabulary.

Tip: listen once for the gist, then again and note three reporting verbs you hear (claim, deny, reveal...).

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "Reported speech: say and tell"

A short, clear video that explains backshift and the difference between say and tell, with plenty of examples to copy out loud.

Tip: after watching, report five things people said to you today using "said that" and "told me".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Connecting Ideas — Discourse Markers

Series
Brainchild — Netflix: "Social Media"

A fun, fast science show for teenagers that builds clear arguments step by step. Great for hearing how a strong opinion is organized with examples and contrasts.

Tip: pause when they change idea and say which connector fits (however, moreover, for instance, therefore).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
6 Minute English — BBC: "How to win an argument"

A short, clear podcast about building and connecting ideas in a discussion. Perfect B2 listening that reuses today's discourse markers.

Tip: listen once for the gist, then again and write down four connectors you hear (however, moreover, therefore...).

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "Linking words and connectors"

A short, clear video that explains how to join ideas with however, moreover, therefore and in addition, with plenty of examples to copy out loud.

Tip: after watching, say your opinion about homework using four different connectors.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Getting Things Done — Causative have/get

Series
The Repair Shop — BBC: any episode

Real experts repair and restore broken objects for their owners. Perfect for hearing the language of services: repair, replace, restore and inspect, used naturally.

Tip: every time an owner gets something fixed, say it with the causative: "She had her clock repaired."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
6 Minute English — BBC: "The right to repair"

A short, clear B2 podcast about repairing things instead of replacing them. It reuses today's service vocabulary in a real-world topic for teenagers.

Tip: write down four service words you hear (repair, replace, fix, technician), then make a causative sentence with each.

Listen on BBC ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "Causative: have/get something done"

A short, clear grammar video that shows exactly how to use have and get something done, with plenty of service examples to copy out loud.

Tip: after watching, describe five things you had done this month using have or get.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Bring It All Together — Mini-presentation

Series
Abstract: The Art of Design — Netflix: any episode

Each designer presents their work in clear, structured talks. Great for hearing how confident speakers open, transition between ideas and recap a key takeaway.

Tip: every time a speaker changes topic, note the transition they use, then copy it for your own outline.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
TED Talks Daily — any short talk under 8 minutes

Short TED talks are perfect models of mini-presentations: a clear outline, signpost phrases and one strong takeaway, all at a B2-friendly pace.

Tip: write down the speaker's outline (three main points) and their final takeaway, then say them out loud.

Listen on TED ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "Signposting language for presentations"

A clear, short video that teaches the exact signpost phrases from this lesson: how to open, move on, highlight a point and sum up with a takeaway.

Tip: after watching, record a one-minute talk using "Let me start with", "Moving on to" and "To sum up".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Take the Stage — Phase 1 Showcase

Series
Shark Tank — any episode

Entrepreneurs pitch, then face a tough Q&A from the investors. A perfect model of acknowledging hard questions, giving concise answers and keeping your composure under pressure.

Tip: each time an investor asks a question, pause the video and predict the response phrase the speaker should use.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
TED Talks Daily — listen to the Q&A after a talk

Many TED interviews end with the host asking questions. Notice how speakers acknowledge a question, keep answers concise and elaborate only when asked a follow-up.

Tip: write down two response phrases you hear, then use them when you rehearse your own Q&A.

Listen on TED ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "Dealing with questions in presentations"

A short, clear video on the exact response phrases from this lesson: how to acknowledge, ask someone to rephrase, answer concisely and defer a question politely.

Tip: after watching, record a one-minute talk and answer two questions you imagine an audience would ask.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Back in the Day — Childhood Memories

Series
Stranger Things — Season 1

Set in the 1980s, this show is pure nostalgia: old-fashioned bikes, walkie-talkies and arcades. Listen for how the kids talk about what they "used to" do and where they grew up.

Tip: every time you spot a throwback detail, pause and describe it with "they used to..." or "they would...".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
The Moth — childhood memory stories

Real people tell true stories, and many reminisce about their childhood. Notice how they set the scene with "used to" and describe repeated habits with "would".

Tip: write down two sentences with "used to" and two with "would" that you hear, then reuse the pattern in your own story.

Listen on The Moth ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "used to, would & past simple"

A short, clear video on exactly this lesson's grammar: when to use "used to" for habits and states, and when to use "would" for repeated past actions.

Tip: after watching, record five sentences about your childhood, mixing "used to" and "would".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — I Wish Things Were Different — Wishes & Regrets

Series
The Good Place — Season 1

A smart, funny show about second chances and regrets. The characters constantly talk about what they wish they had done differently. Listen for "I wish I had..." and "if only".

Tip: every time a character expresses a regret, pause and rephrase it with "I wish I had..." in your own words.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
TED Talks Daily — episodes on regret & desire

Speakers reflect on the choices they wish they had made and the desires that drive them. Notice how they mix "wish + past perfect" for regrets with "wish + past simple" for the present.

Tip: write down two sentences with "I wish I had..." and one with "I wish I were...", then say them out loud.

Listen on TED ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "wish & if only"

A short, clear video on exactly this lesson's grammar: wish + past simple for the present, wish + past perfect for regrets, and wish + would for annoying habits.

Tip: after watching, record five sentences about your own wishes and regrets, mixing all three patterns.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — What Could Have Happened — Modal Perfects

Series
How I Met Your Mother — Season 1

The whole show is one big look back at the past. The narrator constantly explains what he should have done, what could have happened, and how a single choice changed everything. Listen for "should have", "could have" and "would have".

Tip: every time a character talks about a past choice, pause and rephrase it with a modal perfect in your own words.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
TED Talks Daily — episodes on decisions & mistakes

Speakers look back at choices and explain what they should have done and how things could have turned out differently. Notice how they mix "should have" for criticism with "would have" for imagined results.

Tip: write down two sentences with "I should have..." and one with "I would have... if...", then say them out loud.

Listen on TED ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "modals of deduction in the past"

A short, clear video on exactly this lesson's grammar: should have for criticism, could have for missed chances, and might/may have for guesses about the past.

Tip: after watching, record five sentences about a real event, one for each modal: should, could, might and would have.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Make It Happen — The Passive Voice

Series
How It's Made — any episode

Every episode describes how a product is built, step by step, almost entirely in the passive voice. Listen for "is assembled", "are inspected", "is monitored" and "is shipped".

Tip: pause after each step and repeat it as a passive sentence in your own words.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
TED Talks Daily — episodes on how things are designed

Speakers explain how systems and products are designed, tested and improved. Notice how the passive keeps the focus on the process, not the person, and where "by" is added.

Tip: write down three passive sentences from the talk, one in the past with "by + doer".

Listen on TED ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "the passive voice"

A short, clear video on exactly this lesson's grammar: how to form the passive with be + past participle, and when to drop or keep the doer with "by".

Tip: after watching, describe how your phone is updated using five passive sentences.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — The People Who Make It Work — Relative Clauses

Series
Abstract: The Art of Design — any episode

Each episode profiles a creator, the person whose work shaped a field. Listen for relative clauses describing people and the studios where they work: "the designer who...", "a vision which...".

Tip: pause and rebuild each profile as one sentence with a relative clause (defining or non-defining).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
How I Built This — founder stories

Founders tell the story of the people who helped them build a company. Notice how speakers introduce a person and then add a relative clause about them, with or without commas.

Tip: write three sentences from the episode, one defining (no commas) and one non-defining (with commas).

Listen on NPR ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "relative clauses"

A short, clear video on exactly this lesson's grammar: how to use who, which, that, where and whose, and when defining and non-defining clauses take commas.

Tip: after watching, describe the people behind your favorite app using five relative clauses.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Said and Done — Reported Questions

Series
Hot Ones — any celebrity interview episode

The host asks sharp, unexpected questions and the guests answer between spicy wings. Perfect for noticing how a question is asked, then how you would retell it later as a reported question.

Tip: pause after each question and retell it: "He asked whether...", "She wondered what...".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
The Tim Ferriss Show — long-form interviews

An interviewer who probes deeply and asks careful follow-up questions. Notice how he inquires, wonders and refers to earlier answers, and report three questions in your own words.

Tip: write three reported questions from the episode, one yes/no with "whether".

Listen on tim.blog ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "reported questions"

A short, clear video on exactly this lesson's grammar: how to report yes/no questions with if/whether and wh-questions, using statement order and no question mark.

Tip: after watching, report five questions someone asked you this week.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — To Play or Playing? — Gerund vs Infinitive

Series
Stranger Things — any episode

The teen characters constantly talk about what they love doing, avoid doing and plan to do. Perfect for noticing gerunds after enjoy, hate and avoid, and infinitives after want, decide and need.

Tip: pause and write one sentence about each character: "He enjoys...", "She has decided to...".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
The Allusionist — a fun language podcast

A relaxed host who talks about what she enjoys exploring and what she has decided to research. Notice how she keeps on choosing the right form after liking and planning verbs.

Tip: write three sentences from the episode, one with a gerund and one with an infinitive.

Listen on theallusionist.org ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "gerunds and infinitives"

A short, clear video on exactly this lesson's grammar: which verbs take -ing, which take to + verb, and why we always use the gerund after a preposition.

Tip: after watching, write five sentences about your week, mixing gerunds and infinitives.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Looking Ahead — Future Continuous & Future Perfect

Series
Avatar: The Last Airbender — any episode

The characters constantly talk about what they will be doing and what they will have achieved before the next battle. Perfect for noticing the future continuous and the future perfect in action.

Tip: pause and write one sentence about each character: "He will be training..." and "By the finale she will have learned...".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Science Vs — an episode about the future

The host predicts what life will be like and what we will have changed by a certain year. A great way to hear future continuous and future perfect used to talk about real predictions.

Tip: write three sentences from the episode, one with "will be + -ing" and one with "will have + past participle".

Listen on gimletmedia.com ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "future continuous and future perfect"

A short, clear video on exactly this lesson's grammar: when to use will be + -ing, when to use will have + past participle, and which time markers go with each.

Tip: after watching, write five sentences about your year ahead, mixing both future forms.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Caught in the Moment -- Participle Clauses

YouTube
"Participle Clauses Explained" -- English with Lucy / BBC Learning English

A clear video on how to join two actions into one smooth sentence using -ing and past participle clauses. Watch how native speakers use them to tell vivid stories.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três frases que comecem com um participle clause (por exemplo, "Sitting...", "Drenched...", "Stunned by...").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
"Ted Lasso" -- any match scene (Apple TV)

A fun, easy-to-follow show about a football team. Watch a big match scene and notice how commentators and characters describe the action in the moment -- perfect input for participle clauses.

Dica: escolha uma cena de jogo e descreva-a depois em voz alta usando dois participle clauses (por exemplo, "Sprinting forward, he...").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
"6 Minute English" -- BBC -- episodes on sport and big events

A friendly podcast for B1-B2 learners. Listen to short, clear stories about exciting events -- great input to review feeling words like thrilled, stunned, overwhelmed, and exhausted in context.

Dica: ouça uma vez sem texto e uma vez com a transcrição. Anote toda palavra de sentimento e tente reescrever uma frase como participle clause.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Lesson 20 — The Power of Storytelling — The Past Perfect

Series
Stranger Things — any episode

The narrators constantly explain what had happened before the next mystery began. A gripping plot full of suspense, flashbacks and twists — perfect for noticing the past perfect in real storytelling.

Tip: pause and write one sentence with the past perfect: "By the time they arrived, the creature had already..." and spot one twist.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
This American Life — any story episode

Real people tell real stories, setting the scene and reaching a turning point. The narrators use the past perfect again and again to show what had happened before. A great way to hear natural storytelling.

Tip: write three past-perfect sentences you hear, like "She had never seen the ocean before that day".

Listen on thisamericanlife.org ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "the past perfect"

A short, clear video on exactly this lesson's grammar: when to use had + past participle to show which past action happened first, and how it makes your stories richer.

Tip: after watching, write your own eight-sentence story with at least three past-perfect sentences.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Warm-up Key Words The Pattern The Conversation Sound First Your Turn Level Complete
Lesson 1 — Identity & Self-Expression

Level Up

B2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: Warm-up

Beyond the Screen

"If someone had never met you, what three things would you want them to know?"
"What is something about you that most people do not expect?"
Chapter 1: Warm-up

What Makes You You?

"What have you been spending most of your free time on lately?"
"Do you think people see the real you, or do they only see part of who you are?"
Chapter 2: Key Words

Unlocking Words

Ten words to describe who you really are

Vocabulary

Words 1–4

to be easily noticed because of being different or better

stand out

to be easily noticed because of being different or better
"He always stands out because of his unique style."
to understand or solve something after thinking carefully

figure out

to understand or solve something after thinking carefully
"I need to figure out how to beat this level."
to move or progress at the same speed as someone

keep up with

to move or progress at the same speed as someone or something
"It is hard to keep up with all the new updates."
to give others a particular impression of yourself

come across as

to give others a particular impression of yourself
"She comes across as very confident in interviews."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5–8

willing to consider new and different ideas

open-minded

willing to consider new and different ideas
"Being open-minded helps you learn from different cultures."
having a strong desire to win or be better than others

competitive

having a strong desire to win or be better than others
"He is very competitive when it comes to FIFA matches."
a particular way of thinking about something

perspective

a particular way of thinking about something
"Traveling gives you a new perspective on life."
to think someone or something is less capable than they really are

underestimate

to think someone or something is less capable than they really are
"Never underestimate a quiet person."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 9–10

feeling worried about how others perceive you

self-conscious

feeling worried about how others perceive you
"Many teenagers feel self-conscious about speaking in public."
to overcome a barrier or challenge successfully

break through

to overcome a barrier or challenge successfully
"She finally broke through her fear of public speaking."
0 / 2 words revealed
Chapter 3: The Pattern

The Pattern

Discover the grammar behind the sentences

Grammar Discovery

What Is the Pattern?

Look at the highlighted words. What do they have in common?

Mark has lived in São Paulo his entire life.
He has been playing soccer since he was five.
He has watched hundreds of YouTube videos in English.
He has been trying to express himself more confidently.
TenseUseExample
Present Perfect SimpleResult / Completed actionHe has watched hundreds of videos.
Present Perfect SimpleLife experienceHe has lived here his whole life.
Present Perfect ContinuousOngoing process / DurationHe has been playing since he was five.
Present Perfect ContinuousRecent ongoing activityHe has been trying to express himself.
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I have been knowing him for years."
"I have known him for years."

State verbs (know, believe, want, like, own, belong) do NOT use the continuous form.

Grammar Practice

Fill in the Gap

I ___ (play) Minecraft for three years now. ???have been playing
She ___ (visit) Japan twice in her life. ???has visited
We ___ (wait) for the bus for twenty minutes. ???have been waiting
He ___ (never / try) Korean food before. ???has never tried
Chapter 4: The Conversation

The Conversation

Mark meets Yuki at a gaming café

Dialogue

At the Gaming Café

Y
Yuki
"Hey! Are you waiting for a tournament spot too? I am Yuki. I just moved here from Tokyo."
M
Mark
"Yeah! I am Mark. I have been coming here every weekend. How long have you been in Brazil?"
Y
Yuki
"Only three months. I have been trying to make friends, but it is hard when you are new. What do you usually play?"
M
Mark
"Mostly FIFA and Minecraft. People sometimes underestimate me because I come across as quiet, but I am actually really competitive."
Y
Yuki
"I get that! I am the same way. People think I am shy, but once I start playing, I really stand out. What makes you stand out?"
M
Mark
"I think my perspective is different. I am half Japanese, half Brazilian, so I see things from both cultures. It helps me figure out strategies that other people miss."
Y
Yuki
"That is so cool! I have been looking for someone open-minded to team up with. Want to play together?"
M
Mark
"Definitely! I have been wanting to break through to the next level. Let us do this!"
Comprehension Check

About Yuki

1. Where is Yuki from, and how long has she been in Brazil?

Yuki is from Tokyo. She has been in Brazil for three months.

2. What has Yuki been struggling with since she moved?

She has been trying to make friends, but it is hard when you are new.

3. What has Yuki been looking for?

She has been looking for someone open-minded to team up with.

Chapter 5: Sound First

Sound First

Train your ears before you speak

Listening 1

Who Is Speaking?

Listen to someone introducing themselves. What do you learn about them?

0:000:00

What has this person been doing for a long time?

They have been playing competitive games and learning new strategies for years.

Listening 2

A Personal Journey
