Listen to someone talking about personal growth. What have they been working on?

0:000:00

What has this person been working on recently?

They have been working on expressing themselves more and breaking through their comfort zone.

Chapter 6: Your Turn

Your Turn

From guided to free — show what you have learned

Quick Fire

Quick Challenge

0 / 4
Challenge 1 / 4
Someone asks you: "What have you been doing lately?" Respond using Present Perfect Continuous.
"I have been playing a lot of FIFA lately, and I have also been trying to improve my English."
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"I have been knowing him since primary school."
"I have known him since primary school." (know = state verb, no continuous)
"She has been visiting Japan three times."
"She has visited Japan three times." (three times = quantity, use simple)
"I play soccer since I was five."
"I have been playing soccer since I was five." (since = present perfect needed)
"He has been finishing his homework already."
"He has already finished his homework." (already = completed action, use simple)
0 / 4 errors found
Role-play: Guided

Level 1: Guided

Scenario

A new exchange student (your teacher) just arrived at your school. Introduce yourself and tell them about your interests using these keywords:

Mark 13 Morumbi soccer FIFA competitive
Role-play: Semi-free

Level 2: Semi-free

Scenario

You meet someone your age at an international gaming event. They ask: "What makes you different from other players?" Respond naturally.

stand out perspective open-minded
Role-play: Free

Level 3: Free

Scenario

You are recording a 1-minute video introducing yourself for a student exchange program application. Tell the committee who you really are, what makes you unique, and what you have been doing to grow as a person. No keywords — speak freely!

Error Correction

Let Us Fix This

Errors from today's lesson (teacher writes below):

Chapter 7: Level Complete

Level Complete

Let us see what you unlocked today

What I Learned

Today I Can...

Introduce myself beyond basic information using rich vocabulary
Use Present Perfect Simple to talk about results and experiences
Use Present Perfect Continuous to talk about ongoing activities
Use vocabulary like stand out, perspective, and break through confidently
Have a natural conversation about identity and self-expression

Badge Earned!

Level Up — Identity & Self-Expression

Homework

Your Mission

Record a 2-minute "About Me" audio using at least 5 new vocabulary words
Use both Present Perfect Simple and Continuous in your recording
Complete the Pre-class activities for Lesson 2
Coming Up Next

Next Level: What Makes Me Different

Mixed tenses review + personality quiz discussion

Lesson Complete

Level 1 — Complete.

Identity & Self-Expression — Unlocked

Lesson 02 — Chapter 1

What Makes Me Tick

Personality, character, and the tenses we use to talk about who we are

Warm-up

Let's talk about personality

If you could change one personality trait about yourself, what would it be?
Do you think personality is something you are born with, or something that is shaped by experience?
Who do you get along with the most at school? Why?
Chapter 2 — Key Words

Click on each card to reveal

very determined and motivated to succeed

driven

very determined and motivated to succeed
"She is a very driven person."
relaxed and not easily worried

laid-back

relaxed and not easily worried about things
"My dad is really laid-back."
to think about something too much

overthink

to think about something too much or for too long
"I tend to overthink simple decisions."
to have a friendly relationship with someone

get along with

to have a friendly relationship with someone
"I get along with most people at school."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Key Words

More words to discover

to admire and respect someone

look up to

to admire and respect someone
"I look up to my older brother."
able to understand others' feelings

empathetic

able to understand and share the feelings of others
"Empathetic people make great friends."
a particular quality in someone's character

trait

a particular quality in someone's character
"Honesty is one of his best traits."
to influence the way something develops

shape (verb)

to influence the way something develops
"Your experiences shape who you become."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 3 — The Pattern

Spot the pattern

He is very driven and ambitious.
She joined the club last year.
He has participated in three debates so far.
I have been trying to be more confident lately.

What do the highlighted structures have in common?

TenseWhen to use
Present SimplePermanent qualities: He is driven.
Past SimpleFinished actions: She joined last year.
Present PerfectPast + now: He has participated in 3 debates.
Pres. Perf. ContinuousStarted in past, still going: I have been trying...
Chapter 3 — Common Mistake

Common Mistake

"I have joined the club last year."
"I joined the club last year."

With specific time markers (last year, yesterday, in 2020), always use Past Simple.

Chapter 3 — Practice

Fill in the blank

She _____ always _____ very empathetic.?has ... been
He _____ his personality a lot since moving to a new school.?has changed
They _____ along with each other at the party yesterday.?got
I _____ _____ _____ to be less anxious lately.?have been trying
Chapter 4 — The Conversation

Mark & Leo talk about personality

L
Leo
Hey Mark, do you think you are more driven or laid-back?
M
Mark
Hmm, I think I am laid-back most of the time, but I can be driven when it comes to soccer or gaming.
L
Leo
That makes sense. I look up to you because you always stay calm under pressure. That is a great trait.
M
Mark
Thanks! I think being empathetic helps too. I always try to understand how my teammates feel.
L
Leo
Do you think our experiences have shaped our personalities?
M
Mark
Definitely. I used to overthink everything, but playing soccer has taught me to trust my instincts.
Chapter 4 — Comprehension

Did you catch that?

What trait does Leo admire in Mark?
Leo admires that Mark stays calm under pressure.
What question does Leo ask about experiences?
Leo asks if their experiences have shaped their personalities.
Does Leo seem driven or laid-back based on the conversation?
Leo seems curious and thoughtful, asking deep questions about personality.
Chapter 5 — Sound First

Listen — Personality Profiles

Listen to someone describing their personality. Focus on the adjectives and tenses used.

0:000:00
What traits did the speaker mention?
The speaker mentioned being driven, empathetic, and sometimes overthinking things.
What tense did they use most when talking about their traits?
They used Present Simple for permanent traits and Present Perfect for recent changes.
Chapter 5 — Sound First

Listen — Change Over Time

Listen to how this person's personality has changed. What shaped them?

0:000:00
How did the person change?
They went from being laid-back and quiet to being more driven and outgoing.
Chapter 6 — Your Turn

Quick Fire

0 / 4
Challenge 1 / 4
Your friend asks: "What kind of person are you?" Describe yourself using at least 3 personality vocabulary words.
"I would say I am driven when it comes to sports, but pretty laid-back in my daily life. I also think I am empathetic because I always try to understand how others feel."
Chapter 6 — Spot the Error

Find the mistake

"I have joined the club last year."
"I joined the club last year." (Past Simple with specific time)
"She is being ambitious since she was a child."
"She has been ambitious since she was a child." (Present Perfect for duration)
"I am looking up to my coach since forever."
"I have looked up to my coach for as long as I can remember."
"He overthinks all the time since he is a kid."
"He has been overthinking since he was a kid."
0 / 4 errors found
Chapter 6 — Role-Play 1: Guided

Describe your personality

A new student at school asks you: "What kind of person are you?" Introduce yourself and describe 3 personality traits. Use present tenses.

drivenlaid-backempatheticget along with
Chapter 6 — Role-Play 2: Semi-free

Talk about someone you look up to

Tell your teacher about someone you admire. Explain what traits they have and how they have shaped who you are. Use mixed tenses.

look up totraitshapeambitious
Chapter 6 — Role-Play 3: Free

Free Discussion

Debate with your teacher: "Is personality something you are born with, or is it shaped by your experiences?" Give your opinion with examples from your own life.

Chapter 6 — Error Correction

Let's fix these

Error 1

Click here to type the error...

Click here to type the correction...

Error 2

Click here to type the error...

Click here to type the correction...

Error 3

Click here to type the error...

Click here to type the correction...

Chapter 7 — Level Complete

What I Learned

I can use personality vocabulary (driven, laid-back, empathetic, trait, shape)
I can use mixed tenses correctly when talking about personality
I know when to use Present Perfect vs Past Simple
I can describe someone I look up to and explain why
I can discuss whether personality is shaped by experience

Personality Expert

Lesson 2 Badge Earned

Coming Up Next

Lesson 3: The People Around Me

Relative clauses, family, friends, and the bonds that connect us

Lesson Complete

Level 2 — Complete.

Personality & Mixed Tenses — Unlocked

Lesson 03 — Chapter 1

The People Around Me

Relationships, connections, and the clauses that describe them

Warm-up

Let's talk about connections

Who is someone you have a strong bond with? What makes that relationship special?
Do you take after anyone in your family? In what way?
Have you ever fallen out with a friend? What happened?
Chapter 2 — Key Words

Click on each card to reveal

a strong connection between people

bond

a strong connection or relationship between people
"The bond between them is unbreakable."
a brother or sister

sibling

a brother or sister
"Do you have any siblings?"
to annoy or irritate someone

get on someone's nerves

to annoy or irritate someone
"My little brother gets on my nerves."
to depend on someone

count on

to depend on someone, to trust them to help
"You can always count on me."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Key Words

More words to discover

to have an argument and stop being friendly

fall out with

to have an argument and stop being friendly
"I fell out with my best friend over something silly."
a person whose behavior is imitated by others

role model

a person whose behavior is imitated by others
"Athletes can be great role models."
giving help and encouragement

supportive

giving help, encouragement, or emotional support
"My parents are very supportive."
having strong close relationships within a group

close-knit

having strong, close relationships within a group
"We are a very close-knit family."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 3 — The Pattern

Spot the pattern

My brother, who is 15, plays soccer.
The friend that I count on is Leo.
My mother, who is always supportive, helped me.
The bond that we share is very strong.

What is the difference between the sentences with commas and without commas?

TypeRule
Defining (no commas)ESSENTIAL info: The friend that I count on...
Non-defining (commas)EXTRA info: My brother, who is 15,...
who / thatFor people
which / thatFor things (that = defining only)
Chapter 3 — Common Mistake

Common Mistake

"My brother, that is 15, plays soccer."
"My brother, who is 15, plays soccer."

In non-defining relative clauses (with commas), always use who or which, never that.

Chapter 3 — Practice

Fill in the blank

My mother, _____ is always supportive, helped me.?who
The person _____ I trust the most is my brother.?who / that
Soccer, _____ is his favorite sport, taught him teamwork.?which
He has a friend _____ brother is a pro gamer.?whose
Chapter 4 — The Conversation

Mark & Ana talk about family

A
Ana
Hey Mark! Do you think we come from a close-knit family?
M
Mark
Definitely! Everyone says I take after my dad. Do you think you take after anyone?
A
Ana
People say I take after my mom. She is my biggest role model. She is so supportive of everything I do.
M
Mark
That is great. My siblings sometimes get on my nerves, though.
A
Ana
Ha! I know what you mean. But you can always count on family, right? The bond is always there.
Chapter 4 — Comprehension

Did you catch that?

Who does Ana take after?
Ana takes after her mom.
Who is Ana's biggest role model?
Ana's biggest role model is her mom, who she describes as very supportive.
What does Ana say about family bonds?
Ana says you can always count on family because the bond is always there.
Chapter 5 — Sound First

Listen — Family Bonds

Listen to someone describing their family relationships.

0:000:00
How many relative clauses did you hear?
The speaker used several, such as "my sister, who lives abroad..." and "the friend that I trust the most..."
Chapter 5 — Sound First

Listen — Friends vs Acquaintances

What makes a real friend different from an acquaintance?

0:000:00
What is the difference between a friend and an acquaintance according to the speaker?
A friend is someone you have a deep bond with and can count on, while an acquaintance is someone you know but do not share a close relationship with.
Chapter 6 — Your Turn

Quick Fire

0 / 4
Challenge 1 / 4
Describe your best friend using a defining relative clause. Start with: "My best friend is the person who..."
"My best friend is the person who I can always count on. He is someone who never gets on my nerves."
Chapter 6 — Spot the Error

Find the mistake

"My brother, that is 15, plays soccer."
"My brother, who is 15, plays soccer." (non-defining = who, not that)
"The person which I trust is my mom."
"The person who I trust is my mom." (who for people, not which)
"My friend who, lives nearby, helps me."
"My friend, who lives nearby, helps me." (comma before who, not after)
"I have a friend that his brother is famous."
"I have a friend whose brother is famous." (whose for possession)
0 / 4 errors found
Chapter 6 — Role-Play 1: Guided

Describe a family member

Describe a family member to your teacher. Use at least 2 relative clauses (one defining, one non-defining).

whosiblingtake afterclose-knit
Chapter 6 — Role-Play 2: Semi-free

Talk about a friendship

Tell your teacher about a time you fell out with a friend or a sibling who got on your nerves. What happened? How did you resolve it?

fall out withget on nervesbondcount on
Chapter 6 — Role-Play 3: Free

Free Discussion

Do you think friends can become like family? What makes a bond between friends as strong as a family bond? Discuss freely.

Chapter 6 — Error Correction

Let's fix these

Error 1

Click here to type the error...

Click here to type the correction...

Error 2

Click here to type the error...

Click here to type the correction...

Chapter 7 — Level Complete

What I Learned

I can use relationship vocabulary (bond, sibling, count on, fall out with)
I can use defining relative clauses to specify people/things
I can use non-defining relative clauses to add extra information
I know the difference between who, which, whose, and that
I can describe my relationships using relative clauses

Relationship Builder

Lesson 3 Badge Earned

Coming Up Next

Lesson 4: Online vs Offline

Passive voice, digital life, and how technology shapes our perception

Lesson Complete

Level 3 — Complete.

Relative Clauses & Relationships — Unlocked

Lesson 04 — Chapter 1

Online vs Offline

The digital world, passive voice, and how content is shaped by technology

Warm-up

Let's talk about the digital world

What is the first app you open when you pick up your phone?
Do you think your online personality is the same as your offline personality?
Have you ever taken a break from social media? How did it feel?
Chapter 2 — Key Words

Click on each card to reveal

to select and organize content carefully

curate

to select and organize content carefully
"Feeds are curated by algorithms."
a set of rules a computer follows

algorithm

a set of rules that a computer follows to solve a problem
"The algorithm decides what you see."
a tool that changes appearance or removes unwanted things

filter

to remove unwanted things, or a tool that changes appearance
"Many people use filters online."
to move through content on a screen

scroll through

to move through content on a screen by swiping
"I spend too much time scrolling through my phone."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Key Words

More words to discover

to stop being connected to the internet

disconnect

to stop being connected, especially to the internet
"I like to disconnect on weekends."
the trail of data you leave on the internet

digital footprint

the trail of data you leave behind when using the internet
"Your digital footprint can affect your future."
genuine and real, not fake

authentic

genuine and real, not fake or copied
"Being authentic online is important."
time spent looking at a screen

screen time

the amount of time spent looking at a screen
"My screen time is over six hours a day."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 3 — The Pattern

Spot the pattern

Content is curated by algorithms.
The video was watched by millions.
Your data is collected every day.
The app was designed to be addictive.

In these sentences, the action is more important than who does it.

FormStructure
Present Passive (+)am/is/are + past participle
Present Passive (-)am/is/are + not + past participle
Past Passive (+)was/were + past participle
Past Passive (-)was/were + not + past participle
Chapter 3 — Common Mistake

Common Mistake

"The video seen by millions."
"The video was seen by millions."

Passive voice always needs a form of to be (is/are/was/were) + past participle.

Chapter 3 — Practice

Fill in the blank

Social media feeds _____ _____ by algorithms.?are curated
The app _____ _____ to track your behavior.?was designed
Millions of photos _____ _____ every day.?are shared
The study _____ _____ by researchers last year.?was conducted
Chapter 4 — The Conversation

Mark & Sofia talk about social media

S
Sofia
Mark, have you ever thought about how much your screen time affects your day?
M
Mark
Honestly, yes. I scroll through my phone for hours without realizing it. The content is curated so well that it is hard to stop.
S
Sofia
I know! Everything is filtered to look perfect. Sometimes I wonder if anything online is authentic.
M
Mark
Right. And our digital footprint is tracked by companies. Everything we do online is recorded.
S
Sofia
Maybe we should disconnect more often and log off for a while. Our perception of reality is being shaped by what we see on screens.
Chapter 4 — Comprehension

Did you catch that?

What does Sofia wonder about online content?
Sofia wonders if anything online is authentic because everything is filtered to look perfect.
What does Sofia suggest they should do?
Sofia suggests they should disconnect more often and log off for a while.
What passive voice structure does Sofia use in her last line?
"Our perception of reality is being shaped by what we see on screens." (present continuous passive)
Chapter 5 — Sound First

Listen — Designed to Addict

How are social media apps designed? Listen and find out.

0:000:00
According to the audio, how are apps designed to keep users engaged?
Apps are designed with algorithms that curate personalized content, making it hard to log off.
Chapter 5 — Sound First

Listen — Your Digital Shadow

What happens to your data online? Listen carefully.

0:000:00
Why should you care about your digital footprint?
Because it is seen by future employers, universities, and anyone who searches your name online.
Chapter 6 — Your Turn

Quick Fire

0 / 4
Challenge 1 / 4
Transform this active sentence to passive: "Algorithms curate your social media feed."
"Your social media feed is curated by algorithms."
Chapter 6 — Spot the Error

Find the mistake

"The video seen by millions of people."
"The video was seen by millions of people." (missing auxiliary verb)
"Content are curated by algorithms."
"Content is curated by algorithms." (content = singular = is)
"The app was design to be addictive."
"The app was designed to be addictive." (past participle needed)
"Photos is shared every day on social media."
"Photos are shared every day on social media." (photos = plural = are)
0 / 4 errors found
Chapter 6 — Role-Play 1: Guided

Explain how social media works

Explain to someone who has never used social media how it works. Use passive voice to describe the process.

is curatedare filteredis designedalgorithm
Chapter 6 — Role-Play 2: Semi-free

Digital debate

Debate with your teacher: "Should screen time be limited for teenagers?" Use passive voice and vocabulary from this lesson to support your argument.

screen timedisconnectperceptionauthentic
Chapter 6 — Role-Play 3: Free

Free Discussion

If all social media disappeared tomorrow, how would your life change? What would you miss? What would improve? Discuss freely.

Chapter 7 — Level Complete

What I Learned

I can use digital vocabulary (curate, algorithm, filter, screen time)
I can form present passive sentences (is/are + past participle)
I can form past passive sentences (was/were + past participle)
I can discuss how social media shapes our perception
I can debate about screen time and digital footprints

Digital Thinker

Lesson 5: What Would I Do?

Second conditional, hypothetical scenarios, and creative thinking

Lesson Complete

Level 4 — Complete.

Passive Voice & Digital Life — Unlocked

Day 5 -- What Would I Do?

The Dilemma Game

Impossible choices. Real arguments. Your move.

Warm-up

From Real to Hypothetical

Last class you talked about things that changed you. Let's remember:

"My perspective was changed by that experience."

"Apps are designed to keep us engaged."

Now think:

Those things really happened. But what about the things that have NOT happened? "If you found a wallet with five hundred dollars, what would you do?" Welcome to the second conditional.

Today's Goal

Three Rounds

1. The Words

dilemma, consequence, regret

2. The Grammar

If I found... I would...

3. The Debate

defend your choice

Chapter 2 -- Words of Choice

The Vocabulary of Dilemmas

8 words for impossible choices

Words of Choice

Words 1-4

0 / 4 words revealed

A choice where every option has a problem
Dilemma
A choice where every option has a problem
"It is a real dilemma: tell the truth or protect a friend?"
The result that follows your action
Consequence
The result that follows your action
"Every choice has consequences."
How a situation ends
Outcome
How a situation ends
"Nobody could predict the outcome."
An imagined situation, used to think or test
Scenario
An imagined situation, used to think or test
"Picture this scenario: you find a lost phone."
Words of Choice

Words 5-8

0 / 4 words revealed

To pause because you are not sure
Hesitate
To pause because you are not sure
"I would not hesitate for a second."
Attractive — even when you know it is wrong
Tempting
Attractive — even when you know it is wrong
"Keeping the money would be tempting."
A rule you follow because you believe in it
Principle
A rule you follow because you believe in it
"It is against my principles to lie."
The sad feeling about something you did — or did not do
Regret
The sad feeling about something you did — or did not do
"I would regret it forever."
Pronunciation Drill

Say It Like a Debater

Dilemma
Consequence
Hesitate
Principle
Chapter 3 -- The Grammar of Imagination

Second Conditional

If + past simple, would + verb

Discover the Rule

Listen and Notice

"If I found a wallet, I would return it."

"What would you do if you saw a friend cheating?"

"If I were you, I would tell the teacher."

"I would not hesitate if the outcome were clear."

PartFormExample
Condition (imaginary)If + past simpleIf I found a wallet...
Resultwould + base verb...I would return it.
Special: to bewere (all persons)If I were you / If she were rich

Past form, but NOT about the past — about an imaginary present or future. The past creates distance from reality.

Common Mistake

Right vs Wrong

✗ WRONG

"If I would find a wallet..."

✓ RIGHT

"If I found a wallet..."

✗ WRONG

"If I was you, I would stop."

✓ RIGHT

"If I were you, I would stop."

Grammar Practice

Complete the Hypothesis

"If I _____ a million dollars, I would travel the world." (win) [show]

"What _____ you do if you saw a friend cheating?" [show]

"If I _____ you, I would not hesitate." (be) [show]

"Keeping the money would be _____, but wrong." (attractive) [show]

"I would _____ it forever if I lied." (feel bad about) [show]

Chapter 4 -- The Dilemma Cards

Pick a Card

Every card is a trap. Choose anyway.

Artifact

Dilemma Card #07

THE DILEMMA GAME

CARD #07 — THE WALLET

You find a wallet on the school bus. Inside: five hundred dollars in cash, no ID, no phone number. Nobody saw you pick it up.

What would you do?

consequence · principle · regret

Chapter 5 -- Sound First

Listen -- The Wallet Experiment

What do people really do when they find money? Listen and find out.

0:000:00
According to the host, what did most people do with the wallets?
Most people returned them — and wallets WITH money were returned more often.
Why does the host say people returned the money?
Because of how they would feel about themselves — they did not want the regret.
Listening 2

Sofia's Defense

A model answer: choice + consequence + principle.

0:000:00
What would Sofia do if she could read minds?
She would NOT use the power — she would turn it off.
What consequence does she mention?
If she knew everyone's thoughts, she would lose her real friendships.
The Debate

Mark vs Sofia

S
Okay, card seven: if you found a wallet with five hundred dollars, what would you do? Be honest!
M
Honestly? Keeping it would be tempting. But I would take it to the school office.
S
Why? Nobody would know. There would be zero consequences.
M
I would know. That is the consequence. If I kept it, I would regret it every time I used that money.
S
Interesting. So it is a question of principle, not fear. New scenario: what if your best friend found it?
M
Ouch. That is a real dilemma. I would not report him... but I would try to convince him to return it.
S
And if he refused? Would you hesitate then?
M
Yes, I would. And that is the honest answer — the outcome would depend on the friendship. Your turn, Sofia!
Comprehension

About Sofia

What argument does Sofia use to test Mark?

Click to reveal

What new scenario does Sofia create?

Click to reveal

What does Sofia conclude about Mark's choice?

Click to reveal

Chapter 6 -- Speed Round

Quick Fire

6 scenarios -- Answer fast!

Quick Fire

Scenario Challenge

1. You win a million dollars. First move?

Show Answer

2. You can be invisible for one day.

Show Answer

3. You see a classmate cheating on a test.

Show Answer

4. You could live in any country.

Show Answer

5. Your friend asks for advice (use "If I were you").

Show Answer

6. You could delete one social media app forever.

Show Answer

Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"If I would find a wallet, I would return it."

Click to correct

"If I will see him, I would say something."

Click to correct

"If I was you, I would stop."

Click to correct

"What you would do if you won?"

Click to correct

Error Correction

Let's Fix These

Errors from today's debate:

Chapter 7 -- The Final Round

Defend Your Choice

Guided > Semi-free > Free

Round 1: Guided

Three What-Ifs

Scenario: Answer three hypothetical questions with full sentences: a superpower, a million dollars, a time machine.

Keyword chips:

If I had... I would... because...
Round 2: Semi-free

Choice + Defense

Scenario: Dilemma: your team would win the championship if you played unfairly — just once, and nobody would see it. Make your choice, defend it with a consequence and a principle, then survive my counter-attack.

Keywords:

I would... because the consequence would be...
Round 3: Free

The Devil's Advocate

Scenario: The hardest game in debate: defend the position you DISAGREE with, for two minutes — what would happen, what the consequences would be, why someone would choose it. Pure hypothesis, pure skill.

No keywords. Argue like a champion!

Chapter 8 -- Wrap-up

What I Learned

I can build hypotheses: "If I found..., I would...".
I can ask "What would you do if...?" and use "If I were you...".
I defended a choice with consequences and principles.
I argued the position I disagree with — devil's advocate.
I know the words: dilemma, consequence, outcome, scenario, hesitate, tempting, principle, regret.

Debater Badge Earned!

Day 5 -- Complete. Next: He Said, She Said.

T
